question: What is one of the critical requirements for optimal engine performance according to the document?
answer: Heat dissipation is one of the most critical considerations in engine design and with an efficient cooling system

prediction.answer: Optimal engine performance requires effective heat dissipation, which can be achieved by augmenting the rate of heat transfer from the coolant, such as through increasing surface area or optimizing airflow.

retrieved_documents.content:
Automobile radiator is one of the types of cross flow heat exchangers that is an important part of vehicle engine. It is normally used as a cooling unit for the engine where mixture of water and glycol is the medium for heat transfer. The fluid (coolant) moves in a closed system from the radiator to the engine where it conducts heat away from the engine parts. The radiator is typically mounted behind the vehicles grille with outside air driven through the radiator by the vehicle's forward motion that is often supplemented by a fan. Thus the radiator transfers the heat from the fluid inside to the atmosphere thereby cooling the engine.


 Vithayasai et. al.[1] analyzed the effect of electric field on the performance of an automobile radiator. In their work,electric field was supplied on the air side of the exchanger. By adjusting the voltage in a specific range it was observed that the unit with electric field pronounced better heat transfer rate 

 especially at a low frontal velocity of air. Oliet et. al. [2]presented a set of parametric studies performed on automotive radiators by means of detailed rating and design of heat exchangers. Their analysis mainly focused on the influence of working conditions on both the fluids (air and coolant) by showing the impact of selected coolant fluid on the rate of heat transfer. They also demonstrated the influence of certain geometric parameters such as fin pitch and louver angle on radiator global performance and significant design conclusions were reported by analyzing the overall behavior of the radiator.Witry et al. [3] introduced aluminum roll bonding technique used in manufacturing wide range of heat exchangers that can help in augmenting heat transfer whilst reducing pressure drops.Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) results were obtained that showed tremendous levels of possible performance improvement on both sides of heat exchanger. Dittus & Boelter [4] discussed the fundamentals involved in the heat transfer from coolant in the simplest type of tubular structure. All the above mentioned techniques were basically aimed at augmenting rate of heat transfer from the coolant which improves the engine performance in tropical conditions.

 In order to augment the rate of heat dissipation, the radiator core consisting of thin cylindrical tubes were changed to helical structure to expose more surface area to the atmosphere. The magnitudes of main parameters which affect the rate of heat transfer such as mass flow rate of the coolant in the tubes and relative velocity of the air with respect to the vehicle were varied in a specific range and variation of the rate of heat dissipation with these parameters was shown graphically. By analyzing these variations, we showed that the rate of heat dissipation could be improved to a great extent.

retrieved_documents.content:
Quantum dots provide an opportunity to investigate the behavior of semiconductors such as CdTe in the finite size regime [1 4]. Due to the quantum confinement these materials have many fundamentally interesting and potentially useful optical properties [5–9]. Highly luminescent semiconductor nanocrystals (NC) have received increasing attention for use in light-emitting devices and tagging application [10–13]. In the early 90s,high quality CdTe nanocrystals have become available by using $\mathrm{C d}(\mathrm{C H}_{3})_{2}$ as the cadmium precursor and the 

 Corresponding author. Present address: Department of Materials Science and Engineering,University of Pennsylvania, 3231 Walnut Street, Philadelphia, PA 19104, USA. Tel.: +1 302 695 3335; fax: +1302 695 1664.
E-mail address:guolong@seas.upenn.edu (G.L. Tan).

 technical grade trioctylphosphine oxide (Tech TOPO)asthereactionsolvent [14–16].Thesynthesisof CdSe and CdTe nanocrystals through the organometallic route has resulted in high quality semiconductor NC for more than 10years [17–21]. However, some key chemicals used in this traditional route are extremely toxic, pyrophoric, explosive and expensive [20,21]. In recent years other wet chemical methods have been employed for the synthesis of CdTe and CdSe $\mathrm{N C^{\prime}s}$ using safe, low cost, inorganic compounds of CdO,$\mathrm{C d}(\mathrm{A C})_{2},$ and $\mathrm{C d}(\mathrm{C O}_{3})$ as cadmium precursors [22,5]. Thiol as the stabilizing agents was also employed for the aqueous synthesis of CdTe NC [16–19]. Recently, a dry physical route, namely mechanical alloying process, has been used to synthesize surface clean CdTe nanocrystals in larger scale [23]. Subsequent capping the surface of the particles with TOP/TOPO resulted in colorful dispersion solution with similar optical properties to those CdTe nanocrystals prepared by wet chemical method [23,5,19].

 Concurrently, the design and characterization of new non-linear optical materials have attracted a great deal of attention with the goal to develop materials possessing large non-linearities and satisfying various technological requirements for photonic device application [24–26]. Several experimental and theoretical studies have shown that semiconductor NC, such as GaAs [27],$\mathrm{C d S}_{x}\mathrm{S e}_{1-x}[28]$ ,PbSe [29], and $\mathrm{S n O}_{2}[30]$ ,possess large third-order optical non-linear susceptibilities and short response times, which suggest their potential application in non-linear photonic devices [31,32]. Glass is a suitable host matrix for the nanocrystals in optical applications including optical switches and memories [28,33,34]. Although investigations of the optical nonlinearity of CdTe NC doped in glass have been reported by degenerate four-wave mixing techniques in the resonance region $(\hbar\omega>E_{\mathrm{g}},E_{\mathrm{g}}$ is the band gap of material)[35,36], the sign of the non-linear refractive index and non-linear absorption coefficient of the CdTe NC was not determined.


 In this paper, the physically as-milled CdTe nanoparticles were capped on the surface by polarization organic ligands of TOP/TOPO, which were then dispersed in hexane. The linear optical properties (i.e. UV to visible transmission) of colorful dispersion solutions of capped CdTe nanocrystals is presented, together with their nonlinear absorption and non-linear refractive index coefficients determined by Z-scan technique with open and closed aperture. Greatly improved third-order non-linear optical properties were observed for these capped CdTe nanoparticles, which were fabricated by mechanical alloying process before capping. The advantage of the physical process for synthesizing CdTe NC is that one can obtain light emitting materials with tunable emission from the ultraviolet (uncapped CdTe NC's)to visible wavelength range (capped CdTe nanocrystals)[23]. Also, the mechanical alloying method provides an economic and effective routine to produce CdTe NC in relatively large quantities.

High purity cadmium (99.99%) and tellurium (99.999%) powders were mixed and sealed in a hardened steel vial with hardened steel balls. Milling balls with different diameters (2–12mm) were used. The ball milling of Cd and Te powders was performed on a SPEX 8000D Mix/miller using a ball to powder mass ratio of 20:1. The stoichiometric amounts of Cd and Te elemental powder with 1:l atomic ratio were then mechanical alloyed to CdTe compound after ball milling for a certain period. Small amounts of as-milled powders were takenoutfromtheyialwithindifferenttimeintervals for structural and optical measurements. The structural phases of the as-milled powders were measured by a Ri

 gaku powder X-ray diffractometer. The microstructure and morphology of capped and uncapped CdTe nanoparticles were observed using a JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscopy operating at 200kV.

 The as-milled CdTe NC for different time periods were subsequently capped on the surface by polarization organic ligands TOP/TOPO or sodium polyphosphate,which were then dispersed in mixture of hexane. Linear UV visible optical absorption measurements were carried out upon the dispersion solution of these capped CdTe nanocrystals.


 Afterwards, the colorful dispersion solutions of the capped CdTe nanoparticles were used for third-order optical non-linearity measurements by Z-scan technique. The colorful solution containing capped CdTe nanoparticles was deposited onto the surface of a microquartz glass slide by a Spin Coater. The samples were coated on the surface of the glass slide in such a way that the surface of the slide is fully covered and no air bubbles were trapped between the solution and substrate interface. The coated glass slides were then put in a small vacuum chamber for drying.


 The third-order non-linear susceptibilities of the coated films containing CdTe NC on the surface of glass slides were measured using a single beam Z-scan technique [25,36]. Using a single Gaussian laser beam in a focused geometry, the transmittances of a non-linear medium as a function of the sample position z with respect to the focal plane are related to the non-linear absorption and refraction of sample for the open and closed aperture geometries, respectively. Therefore the non-linear absorption and non-linear refractive index coefficients can be obtained from the Z-scan measurements. The excitation source was a ∼lps Ti: Sapphire laser operating at a wavelength of ${\sim}800\mathrm{n m}$ l, a repetition rate of 82MHz with a good Gaussian intensity distribution. The laser beam was focused to a waist radius ~57m with a lens of focal length 75mm. A typical peak power density was ${\sim}85\mathrm{k W/c m}^{2}$ . The thickness of the sample was ∼90m, which satisfies the condition of an optically thin sample $(z_{\mathrm{R}}=\pi\omega_{0}/\lambda>L)$ 1. The sample at the vicinity of beam waist was moved to the negative and positive directions of the propagation of laser light.The laser power transmission through the sample was measured with open and closed apertures in the far-field region.

What is the main advantage of the DRRAC?

prediction.answer: The main advantage of DRRAC is that it eliminates fluctuations around singular points by damping unnecessary joint velocities, unlike DARAC which only damps joint accelerations.

retrieved_documents.content:
Sun-Li Wu and Shir-Kuan Lin 

 Institute of Control Engineering,National Chiao Tung University,Hsinchu 300,Taiwan, ROC 

 (Received September 1996; infinal form March 997)

 Abstract: The damped least-squares method has frequently been used to solve the singularity problem of resolved-acceleration control schemes. It works by damping only the joint accelerations, so that the joint accelerations in the degenerated directions (the end-effector cannot move along these directions) are zero at a singular point.However, the joint velocities in the degenerated directions may be nonzero in some situations, in which case they will create fuctuations around the singular point. In this paper, a damped-rate resolved-acceleration control scheme (DRRAC) is proposed to overcome this drawback. The paper also shows that the DRRAC is asymptotically stable, and its convergent property is discussed. Experiments were undertaken to verify the proposed control scheme.Copyright © 1997 Elsevier Science Ltd 

 Keywords: Robot control, resolved-acceleration control, singularities, damped leastsquares method.

A modular integrated robot control system (MIRCS) (Wu and Lin, 1994) is used to imple ment the DARAC and the DRRAC on the PUMA 560 six-joint robot. Because the values of the in ertia parameters are unavailable from the manufacturer, it has been impossible to implement the computed-torque scheme in the laboratory. Instead, a robust independent joint controller (Hsia,et al., 1991; Tsai and Lin, 1990) was used to replace the computed-torque scheme for robot control in the joint space.


 The input commands in all the experiments were step commands. The actuator force of each actuator is constrained by its physical limits, so that saturation will occur if the controller output is large. To remedy the saturation problem, the speed-constraint function (Khatib, 1987) was joined to the controller for the large step command. This function is described as follows:

 a^{*}=K_{D r}\left[-\dot{r}+K_{P r}K_{D r}^{-1}V(r_{d}-r)\right]

 where 

 V=\operatorname*{m i n}(1,\frac{V_{m a x}}{\frac{k_{p r}}{k_{d r}}\parallel r_{d}-r\parallel}).

 $k_{p r}$ and $k_{d r}$ are the diagonal elements of $K_{P},$ .and $K_{D r}$ ,respectively.$V_{m a x}$ is the maximum velocity.Here,$V_{m a x}=0.1~\mathrm{m/s}$ ;. The controller parameters were defined as follows:$K_{P}=64I,K_{D}=16I$ the sampling period was 3 ms.$\boldsymbol{\rho}$ of the DARAC and $\mathbf{\rho}_{r}$ of the DRRAC were both chosen to be the same as eq. (28).


 Experiment 1 illustrates the responses of the manipulator when there was an unreachable region 

 between the desired position and the initial position. The orientation was ignored. The initial and target positions were $(-0.1,\quad0.2,\quad0.8)$ and $(0.15,\quad-0.15,\quad0.8)$ ,respectively; they are illustrated in Fig. 6. The singular circle in the figure is the boundary of the workspace, the unreachable region is inside it, and the workspace of the manipulator is outside it. The positional errors of the DARAC and the DRRAC are shown in Figs 7(a) and (b), respectively. From these two figures,it was found that the positional errors of the two methods had converged to zero, though the convergent rate of the DARAC was faster than that of the DRRAC. After the end-effector had touched the circle, there were some fluctuations around the singular circle when the DARAC was used. For the DRRAC, the trajectory just had one very slight fluctuation, since the unnecessary joint velocities were removed. However, the convergent rate of the DRRAC was slow when the target was close to the singular point. This occurred because the joint velocities along the degenerated directions were damped when the end-effector moved into the neighborhood of the singularity.

 In Experiment 2, the target was outside the workspace of the manipulator. The initial position was $(-0.1,\;0.2,\;0.8)$ Iand the target was $(-0.05,0.05$ 0.8); these are illustrated in Fig. 8. The positional errors of the DARAC and the DRRAC are shown in Fig. 9. When the DARAC was used,after the end-effector had touched the singular circle, fluctuations occurred around the workspace boundary. However, when the DRRAC was used,after the end-effector had touched the circle, the end-effector moved along the workspace boundary without fluctuations. Finally, the end-effector converged to the singular point nearest to the target.

 Experiment 3 illustrates the response of the manipulator in the face of orientation degeneration. The initial position and the target were $(-0.1,0.2,0.8)$ and (-0.0203, 0.1501, 0.8649),respectively. The orientation was held towards the $+\mathbf{Z}$ axis. At the target,$q_{5}$ was zero, i.e., a singular point. The joint displacements of the joints 4, 5,and 6 for the DARAC are shown in Fig. 10(a).In this figure, when $q_{5}=0$ ,joints 4 and 6 rotated at the same speed in the opposite directions. This is because the DARAC forces $\ddot{q}_{4}^{*}$ and $\ddot{q}_{6}^{*}$ to zero when $q_{5}=0;$ ;however,$\dot{\pmb q}_{\pmb4}$ and $\dot{q}_{6}$ were nonzero.The results with the DRRAC are shown in Fig.10(b). When qs approached 0, both joints then stopped at the same time.


 The results of these three experiments show that the proposed method also has satisfactory results even if the perfect computed-torque scheme is not used.

As the 2018 season gets underway, Syngenta stands with you to develop strategies that will help growers succeed.


 oday, "customer-first" is not just a slogan for agribusinesses. lt's a necessity. Lower commodity prices and market volatility are pressuring growers to make hard choices about farming inputs. As a result, they need more of your time and expertise to help them discern the value of every purchase they make. Syngenta is poised to help by being a consistent, reliable partner for you. We're committed to continue delivering exceptional products that clearly demonstrate a return on investment and contribute to a grower's bottom line.

 As this issue of Thrive reflects, the breadth and depth of our portfolio are positively impacting a wide variety of crops, including Trivapro®fungicide in corn, soybeans and wheat and Minecto Pro insecticide in citrus, vegetables and potatoes.


 But it's our people who are bringing our brands to life. Whether they are collaborating in one of our state-of-the-art laboratories or riding with you in your truck,the men and women of Syngenta are engaged in meaningful discussions that go beyond a single product or pest. Our sales and agronomy teams in the field always welcome the opportunity to work directly with you and participate in as many conversations as it takes to make sure growers put the right product on the right acre at the right time. As you'll see on the pages that follow, some of these conversations are taking place at our Grow More"Experience sitesunique learning environments that enable you to see firsthand how our products perform under local growing conditions. Others may involve simpler, more direct talks in your office or at a grower's kitchen table.

 Regardless of the venue, our partnership with you is invaluable. We understand that placing our technologies on individual farms is possible because of you. You understand the limits of a one-size-fits-all approach 

 "We're committed to continue delivering exceptional products that clearly demonstrate a return on investment and contribute to a grower's bottom line.,

 and the benefits of tailoring solutions to meet each farm's unigue needs, You have the ability to transform a complex problem into a simple answer. And the respect it takes to inspire growers to listen.

 You also have our respect—and support. As you work harder to service your customers, we, in turn,will work harder to serve you, by giving you the tools and information you need to thrive. Together, we can bring value to farms across the country—throughout 2018 and beyond.

 LISA MORICLE

Research is the foundation of the Grow More Experience sites.Through trials and demonstrations featuring seeds, seed treatments, crop protection chemistries, cover crops and more, visitors have the unique opportunity to walk around, dig up proof and see the performance of plots featuring the Syngenta product portfolio,in comparison with competitor products and untreated crops.

 Many of the early-season visitors are focused on seed performance.


 "One of the first, most critical decisions growers make each season is seed selection," says Bruce Battles, head of agronomy for Syngenta Seeds. "At the Grow More Experience sites, visitors view hybrids and varieties side-by-side to see how they perform 

 against each other. Growers don't have to make their own investments to test different seed in their fields, We make that investment for them."

 Choosing how to keep the seed protected once it's in the ground is also important, notes Jim McGill, a sales agronomist at Midwest Farmers Coop in Waverly, Nebraska. The trials he observed at the York,Nebraska, Grow More Experience sit in 2017 reinforced this belief.


 "Stand establishment is the basis of growers' success for the whole year," McGill says.


 "For me, the soybean seed treatment trials were eye-opening,because they demonstrated how the right seed treatment can dramatically improve early-season vigor,disease protection and early stand establishment."

 Of course, the work and worry don't stop for growers after they plant their seed. They rely on effective crop protection products for season-long plant vigor. As Grow More Experience site attendees visit the locations at different times throughout the season, the featured crop protection trials change to highlight the most important pest issues for the region at that time.

 "That is what's so valuable about the Grow More Experience sites," says Jeff Laufenberg, Syngenta technical development lead. "Early in the season, visitors learn about seed and seed treatments. As they come back for events throughout the season, they can see crop protection trials, receive pestmanagement education and learn about innovative agronomic 

 best practices before harvest. No two events are the same, because the topics and technologies are constantly evolving."

。s agriculture constantly evolves, growers, resellers and others in the industry may find it challenging to keep up with the latest innovations. Through 

 cutting-edge res More" Experience sites address this challenge head-on.

 Since their inception, these sites—managed by Syngenta agronomists and technical development leads—have acted as local laboratories for thousands of visitors, who have seen firsthand how Syngenta products can help maximize crop productivity. Heading into 2018, nearly 50 Grow More Experience sites, serving prime production areas from Washington to Florida,are maintaining this momentum.

Hong Liu, Zhaohui Pu, Dingquan Xiao, and Jianguo $\mathsf{Z h u^{a)}}$ Department of Materials Science, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China 

 Zhihong Wang, Huidong Huang, and Yanrong Li 
School of Microelectronics and Solid-State Electronics, University of Electronics Science 
and Technology of China, Chengdu 610054, China 


 (Received 5 February 2006; accepted 23 July 2006; published online 17 October 2006)

 Lanthanum doped lead titanate ferroelectric thin films were grown on $\mathrm{Pt/Ti/SiO_{2}/Si(100)}$ substrates using rf magnetron sputtering. X-ray diffraction patterns show that the main phase of the films is tetragonal perovskite with preferred orientation of (111) perpendicular to the substrate. We investigated the ferroelectric domain patterns and the corresponding polarization arrangements of the films using piezoresponse force microscopy. Head-to-tail polarization configurations were often found to exist, indicating that the low electrical energy configurations are quite common in these thin films. Nanoscale bandd $90^{\circ}~a{\textrm{-}}a$ domain patterns as small as 30-60 nm in width were observed. The banded domains alternated with adjacent lamellae can be attributed to mechanical strains at the surface and at the interface between the substrate and the film. © 2006 American Institute of Physics. [DOI: 10.1063/1.2357865]

PLT thin films were prepared on $\mathrm{Pt/Ti/SiO_{2}/Si(100)}$ 1substrates by using rf magnetron sputtering. The optimal preparation conditions were to deposit at 300 °C and sequentially to anneal at 600 C for 1h. XRD characterizations indicated that the films were (111) oriented tetragonal perovskite phase. PFM imaging shows the existence of nanoscale banded a-a 90° ferroelectric domain patterns with 

 lamellae of 30–60 nm in thickness in these films. Alternating banded domain with adjacent lamellae were observed and attributed to the mechanical strains at the surface and at the interface between film and substrate. Head-to-tail polarization configurations were also often observed, indicating that the low electrical energy configurations are quite common in PLT thin films.

Figures 3(a)–3(c) show the simultaneously acquired topography image and the corresponding piezoresponse images of in-plane polarization (IPP) and out-of-plane polarization (OPP) of $2\times2~\mu\mathrm{m}^{2}$ area of the PLT film, respectively.

 The topography image (atomic force microscopy image in contact mode) in Fig. 3(a) shows that the root mean square (rms) roughness of the film is about 4 nm, indicating that the PLT thin films obtained at optimal preparation conditions have rather smooth and dense surface.


 As shown in Figs. 3(b) and 3(c), there is scarcely any OPP in the PLT film as indicated by the approximate zero piezoresponse signal in Fig. 3(c), but banded domain patterns can be clearly seen in Fig. 3(b). Figure 3(d) illustrates the suggested domain patterns for the observed IPP around line $A–A^{\prime}$ in Fig. 3(b). Most of these banded domains can be attributed to a-a 90° domain patterns with the polarization vector lying in the x-y plane from the mapped IPP and geo8,9
metrical considerations.


 Figure 3(b) also clearly shows that parallel stripes alternated between brightness and darkness appears in most of the grains. The bright or dark lamellae correspond to the $a_{1}$ or $a_{2}$ domain, as schematically drawn in Fig. 4,respectively 

 The thickness of lamellae domains can be easily measured by means of line scan piezoresponse signal along lines A-A'and B-B' in Fig. 3(b) crossing the parallel stripes, corresponding to the hills and valleys shown in Figs. 3e) and 3(f), respectively.It is indicated from Figs.3(e) and 3(f) that 

 a-adomainsliceswereabout 1minheight and 30and 60 nm in width. The thicknesses of the domain slices are not the same, because they can be correlated with stress 10
conditions.

question: What is the web address at the bottom of the page?
For what purpose is establishing context indispensable?
answer: To define norms, standards, or thresholds for sustainability performance and measure, manage, and report sustainability activities effectively.

prediction.answer: To define norms, standards, or thresholds for measuring, managing, and reporting sustainability performance.

retrieved_documents.content:
In corporate sustainability management, context - or sustainability context, as the Global Reporting Initiative calls it - refers to the combination of circumstances that determines what the norms, standards or thresholds for sustainability performance should be when attempting to judge whether or not an organization's activities are sustainable.


 The factors involved in determining context include: (a) who an organization's stakeholders are,(b) what its impacts on related vital capitals are or ought to be, (c) what the carrying capacities of the capitals are, and (d) who else, if anyone, is responsible for maintaining the capital resources involved. All of this gives rise to normative impacts, or sustainability standards of performance,against which actual impacts can be measured, managed and reported. Proportionate allocations of available resources and/or duties to preserve, create and maintain them can then be assigned to individual organizations.


 The purpose of establishing context, then, is to make it possible to define norms, standards or thresholds for what an organization's impacts on vital capital resources must be in order for its activities to be sustainable. Actual impacts can then be measured against such benchmarks.Establishing context is therefore an indispensable requirement for measuring, managing and reporting sustainability performance!

M.J.Morris,C. J. Kandiah and E. Burcher2

 1Department of Biological Sciences, Deakin University, Geelong, Victoria, 3217,Australia and 2School of Physiology and Pharmacology, University of NSW,Sydney, NSW,2033, Australia 


 Substance P (SP) can affect blood pressure through central or peripheral actions, evoking pressor responses when injected into the central nervous system, and endothelium dependent peripheral vasodilation and a decrease in blood pressure on systemic administration. The observation of increased SP content in discrete brain areas of the spontaneously hypertensive rat (SHR) (1) suggests a possible involvement of SP in the pathogenesis of genetic hypertension. Little is known concerning the role of peripheral SP in models of genetic hypertension. The aim of this study was to compare SP content in peripheral blood vessels of SHR, their normotensive controls, Wistar Kyoto (WKY) and Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats.Blood vessels examined included abdominal aorta (AA), vena cava (VC), mesenteric artery (MA), portal vein (PV), renal artery (RA) and renal vein (RV).  Animals were anaesthetised (pentobarbitone, 60 mg/kg), exsanguinated, and 1-2 cm segments of each vessel rapidly excised, weighed and snap frozen. Groups of vessels from 5 animals were extracted in acid (efficiency 77%) and lyophylised. SP-like immunoreactivity (SP-LI) was measured by radioimmunoassay using an antiserum to SP showing minimal cross reactivity to other tachykinins (2). Chromatographic characterisation of vessel extracts demonstrated one major peak of immunoreactivity which co-eluted with SP standard.

 Results are shown below, as pg SP-LI/mg tissue $\left(mean\pm SEM,*p<0.05\right)$ 1.

 In all strains the greatest SP-LI concentrations were observed in the MA and PV. Vessels from the two normotensive rat strains contained similar amounts of SP-LI. For all veins examined, and the RA, SHR exhibited significantly lower SP-LI concentrations relative to WKY controls. The lower concentration of SP, a vasodilator peptide, may have implications for the regulation of blood pressure in the SHR. Results of the present study do not reveal the cause of the reduced SP content, which might be due to decreased production or increased release of SP from sensory nerves innervating blood vessels.

 1. Nagashima A, Takano Y,Tateishi K, Matsuoka Y, Hamaoka T and Kamiya H (989).Brain Research,499: 198-203.


 2. Morris J, Gibbins I, Campbell G, Murphy R, Furness J and Costa, M (1986). Cell and Tissue Research, 243: 171-184.

prediction.answer: The focus is on the new register file structure designed for high-speed microprocessor pipeline operations, addressing read-time and write-time errors in conventional two-port RAMs, enabling a 250 ns machine cycle time in a 16-bit microprocessor.

retrieved_documents.content:
HIROSHI KADOTA, SUMIO OZAWA, KATSURA KAWAKAMI, AND EISUKE ICHINOHE 

 Abstract-The pipeline operations of the register file in a microprocessor are analyzed in detail. Conventional register files,two-port static RAM's, have two problems in successive write-to-read operations.1) A read-time error takes place when the transition of the W/R mode and the transition of register address occur simultaneously. 2) A writetime error takes place when the supply voltage is low. A new register file structure is proposed, which has three address word lines and four data bit lines for each memory cell. This structure enables the independent write and read operations to each other, and can solve the two problems. By using this register file structure, a new 16 bit microprocessor with 250 ns machine cycle time is successfully developed.Several other features of this processor are also explained and discussed.

A new register file structure has been developed for a pipeline data operation in a microprocessor. In this structure,read and write operations can be executed in any sequence without errors in the wide range of the supply voltage. Using this register file and the results of the analysis, a 16 bit microprocessor with 250 ns machine cycle time has been designed and successfully fabricated.

It is almost inevitable that two transitions, register address and write-to-read operation, occur simultaneously at the RF in a highly concurrent pipeline-operated processor. To avoid the read-time error without a buffering clock phase, we have adopted a new RF structure. The circuit diagram of the memory cell and the I/O cell of this new RF are illustrated in the solid and bold line part of Fig. 3. The memory cell has two complementary data bit lines, DBL.A and DBL.A,and one address word line, AWL.A only for a write operation. It also has one data bit line,DBL.A(R) or DBL.B(R) and one addressword line, AWL.A(R) or AWL.B(R) only for each read operation of the A-or B-port. In the I/O cell, pull-up transistors and sense amplifiers are connected to the DBL.A(R) and DBL.B(R), and the DBL drivers which will drop the DBL voltage below $V_{w t}$ are connected to DBL.A and DBL.A. In this structure, write and read operations are available almost independently of each other. The voltage of the DBL for the read operation never falls below $V_{w t}$ ,so the read operation can be accomplished correctly. A microphotograph of the memory cell of this new RF is shown in Fig. 5. The cell is $61\times68\mu\mathrm{m}^{2}$ in area, which is about 30 percent larger than a conventional two-port RAM.


 The observation of the new RF operation is carried out by computer circuit simulation and by experimental verification using the test circuit of the new RF with output amplifiers.Fig. 6(a) shows the result of the circuit analysis, and Fig.6(b)is the oscillograph of the input and output waveforms of the test circuit. The time point $ \text{"}X\text{"}$ is the same critical point, and the output waveform of the sense amplifier changes from low level to high level in less than 20 ns after the time $ \text{"}X\text{"}$ This means that the read operation is correctly accomplished within 20 ns access time.


 A memory cell of the two-port RAM has another problem in write operation. Usually, the DBL of the A-port and B-port are connected to the complementary nodes of the memory cell, respectively. When the duration of the write operation $(T_{w t})$ is short and the supply voltage $V_{d d}$ is low, the operation,writing "O" into the memory cell from the A-port and reading out these data from the B-port in the successive cycle,leads to an erroneous result. Fig. 7 shows the voltage transients of the memory cell in this situation, At the time point "Y" when the memory cell is not yet in the stable state,the read gate of the B-port (AWL.B(R)in Fig. 3) turns on. This introduces the trigger which turns over the memory cell state. The $V_{d d}$ dependency of this write-time error is ilustrated in Fig.8. In the 

 case of the $E/E{\tt-t y p e~A W L}$ ,driver (shown by the solid line), the minimum duration for a correct write operation $(T_{w t\cdot\operatorname*{m i n}})$ has its peak at $V_{d d}=4.5\mathrm{~V~}$ . Two contradictory effects are considered to cause this $V_{d d}$ dependency: 1) the stability of the initial state of the memory cell (the node voltage just before the read operation) increases with $V_{d d}$ ,and 2) the trigger energy introduced by the read gate increases with $V_{d d}$ 

 In the region where $V_{d d}$ is less than 4.5 V, the second effect is dominant; on the other hand, in the higher $V_{d d}$ region,the first effect is dominant. As shown in Fig. 8 (dashed line), by using an $E/D{\tt-t y p e}$ :AWL driver, the write operation becomes faster and $T_{w t\operatorname*{m i n}}$ becomes smaller. To avoid this write-time error, we use the $E/D{\mathrm{-t y p e~A W I}}$ ,driver with a little more power dissipation than the $E/E{\mathrm{-t y p e}}$ :driver.

A new 16 bit microprocessor with a 250 ns machine cycle time has been developed. This high speed is achieved mainly by the pipeline operation described in the preceding sections.The chip is primarily designed for control applications. The architecture of this processor is shown in Fig. 9. In addition to the new RF structure, it has other features. These are explained below.


 1) For the pipeline operations of instruction fetch, there are two words of prefetch buffers in the instruction fetch controller (IFC). The word size of the buffer has been decided so that the processor has the highest value of "speed/circuit size"when it implements ordinary control programs. The IFC starts 

 to prefetch if any of the prefetch buffers is empty and the I/O multiplexer (I/O MUX) is not occupied by the data I/O operations. Fig. 1O shows the relationship between the average execution time $(T_{\mathrm{e x}})$ of typical control programs and the word size of the buffers $(N_{\tt b u f f})$ .If the program did not include the branch instruction,$T_{\tt e x}$ would become shorter monotonously with $N_{\mathtt{b u f f}};$ :but in real programs, branch instructions disturb the sequency of instructions, and the prefetched instructions are discarded. In this case, the prefetch operations are not only useless, but also harmful for the execution speed of the processor because they interfere with the data I/O operations inversely. The tradeoff point exists near two words.

 2) The processor is controlled by the microprogram which is divided into two levels. The first one is in the interpreting processor (IP) (the size is 70 words × 20 bits), and the second one is in the microprogram controller (MPC) (the size is 700 words X 27 bits). The two-level structure is useful to economize the storage size of the control memory by about 20-30 percent and to reduce the coding errors of microinstructions. The microprogramming used in this processor is rather vertical, so further decoding is necessary for many microinstructions from the MPC.


 3) A 16 bit timer, an 8 bit serial $\mathbf{I}/\mathbf{O},$ and a clock oscillator are included. These functions make the processor suitable for real-time control applications in compact systems.

 4) In the operation of addressing,a logical 16 bit address in the memory address register (MAR) or program counter (PC)is added to the 4 bit data in the segment base register (SBR).The joint 18 bit physical address is used for the memory access.5) The processor has three levels of maskable interrupts which are controlled in the interrupt block (INTR).

 The chip is successfully fabricated by Si n-channel E/D-MOS 

 technology with a 3 μm design rule. The effective channel length is 2.4 m and the thickness of the gate oxide is 60 nm.

 The photomicrograph of the chip is illustrated in Fig. 11.The chip is $6.6\times7.0~\mathrm{m m^{2}}$ in area with 40 000 transistors.Several LSI characteristics are listed in Table I. The machine cycle time is measured as a function of the supply current $I_{d d}$ The result is illustrated in Fig. 12. This microprocessor has 97

 distinct instructions. The execution times of several instructions are listed in Table II.

The image displays a block diagram labeled "Fig. 1. Block diagram and signal flows of the register file and its peripheral circuits." It illustrates the internal architecture of a computer's register file, showing components such as the ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit), LATCH, DEC (Decoder), IPC (Instruction Pointer/Counter), MPC (Micro Program Counter), and various control signals like INSTRUCTION, OPERAND, and MICRO INSTRUCTION. Signal flows are indicated with arrows connecting these blocks, demonstrating how data and control signals move between components. The diagram is presented in black and white with clear labels for each part.

Postischemic cardiac damage was assessed in perchloric acid extract of rabbits by NMR (9.4Tesla,400 MHz), GroupI $(n=5)$ wcrc controls. Group II $(n=5)$ werc perfused with Univcrsity of Wisconsin solution (UWS) at 4C for 24 hours. Group fII $(n=2)$ were stored for 24 hours at 4C in UWS. After 24 hours, Groups II and IIl were reperfused for 30 minuets at $37^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ Perfusion was assessed with Tl-201 autoradiography and viability with light microscopy. Mean ± SEM values for creatine-phosphocrealinc/lactate were: Group I:$7.82\pm1.5$ g;Group 11:5.9±1.43;Group III: 3.3±0.9. Gradual build-up of lactate and inorganic phosphate indicated progressive myocardial damage. There was no correlation beiween the $\mathtt{C r}/\mathtt{P C r}$ over lactate ratio and the $\mathrm{P\dot{C}r/B-A T P}$ 'ratio. In summary, NMR spectroscopy yields useful information on viable and nonviabie postischemic cardiac mass.


 P-3O MICROVASCULAR COMPETENCE IN ISCHAEMIC HEARTS ARRESTED WITH OXYGENATED CRYSTALLOID CARDIOPLEGIC SOLUTION. Choong YS,Cottier D and Forster K. Department of Pathology,School of Medicine,University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand.

 We have shown that oxygenated St. Thomas Hospital solution (STH) improves post-ischaemic recovery of cardiac function and metabolism while minimizing the elevation of coronary vascular resistance during ischaemia.To examine the pathogenesis of capillary incompetence,isolated working rat hearts were arrested (2min at 4oC) with either STH or fully oxygenated STH. Hearts were then subjected to global ischacmia (multidose cardioplegia every 40 min)for 5 hours at 20c. They were then perfused fixed and injected with nuclear track emulsion (NTE) to identify competent vessels in full transaural resin embedded sections by light microscopy.In $\mathrm{S T H}+\mathrm{O}_{4}$ hearts, greater than 9oz of vessels remained patent and NTE was distributed across the ful1 thickness of the Lv. In contrast,sTH hearts showed ore than 9oz reduction of competent capillaries predominantly in the subendo-myocardium of LV with ischaemic yocytes. This result has important implications for myocardial protection during cardiac surgery.

 (Supported by the National Heart Poundation of New Zealand).

Cast on 87 stitches using size US 3 needle.Starting with purl row: p29, pm, p29, pm, p29.Each marker separates one repeat of the chart.Work the chart rows three times total over all stitches.


 Repeat chart A 9 times over all stitches, slipping each marker to the left needle as you come to them. Bind off.

second US3/3.25mm needle cast on an additional 10 stitches and work Chart B, starting at the top right corner and work counter-clockwise, knitting together lace edge stitches with picked up stitches on every right side row.


 Turning Corners with Knit On Edging The corners are worked with short rows to make for smooth knitting at the corners. Work Chart B up to 2 stitches before first stitch marker. Start all odd rows on the right side of the shawl and purl all even rows. Continue knitting Chart B to 2 stitches before second and third marker, turn corner. Start turning the last corner 4 stitches before the end of picked up stitches, repeating the corner turn twice over four stitches. Seam edges together. Weave in ends, block.

retrieved_documents.content:
The purpose of this study was to examine the effects of an acute transition from RFS to FFS running in habitual RFS runners on GRF as well as ankle and knee joint variables at two different speeds. Foot strike patterns were confirmed using the SI, with RFS patterns producing index values lower than 30% and imposed FFS pattern showing index values greater than 100% (Paquette et al., 2013).The results showed changes in sagittal ankle and knee joint kinetics that occur when an FFS is imposed on habitual RFS runners, regardless of running speed (i.e. slow or fast). These findings in competitive, high-mileage male runners are consistent with previous research conducted in different populations (e.g. mixed genders, low mileage, noncompetitive, non-runners and single speed).

 It was expected that a potential interaction of speed and foot strike existed for ankle and knee joint kinetics. This potential interaction was expected as metabolic cost during running is greater during imposed FFS compared to habitual RFS at slow $(\mathrm{i.e.}\quad3.0\quad\mathrm{m}\quad\mathrm{s}^{-1}\quad\mathrm{o r}\quad{\sim}8.9\quad\mathrm{m i n}\quad\mathrm{m i l e}^{-1})$ and medium $(\mathrm{i.e.~}3.5\;\mathrm{m~s}^{-1}\;\mathrm{o r}\sim7.7\;\mathrm{m i n}\;\mathrm{m i l e}^{-1})$ but this difference is absent at a faster speed $(\mathrm{i.e.}\;4.0\;\mathrm{m}\;\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ or ${\sim}6.7$ min mile), which was still slower than the fast speed used in the current study (Gruber et al., 2013).At faster speeds, the similar metabolic cost appears to be the result of an increase in metabolic cost in RFS.However, the current results showed no interaction between habitual RFS and imposed FFS and running speed for GRF and joint kinetic variables.

 As expected, a number of foot strike main effects were found for kinetic and kinematic variables. The 

 Notes: #Significant foot strike main effect.*Significant speed main effect.

 current finding that the LR was greater in RFS running compared to imposed FFS is consistent with previous findings (Giandolini et al., 2013; Lieberman et al., 2010). Greater LRs have been linked to running related injuries, particularly tibial stress fractures (Milner et al., 2006; Zadpoor & Nikooyan,2011). Although still no causal relationships have been established, the greater LR found observed in RFS may be related to the higher injury rates observed in habitual RFS runners (Daoud et al.,2012).


 Despite the fact that LR was greater in RFS,maximal vertical GRF was greater in imposed FFS.The greater maximal vertical GRF in combination with the more anterior COP location (i.e. greater SI)appears to be responsible for the larger ankle plantarflexor moment found during imposed FFS compared to habitual RFS (Kulmala et al., 2013; Rooney &Derrick, 2013; Stearne et al., 2014). The greater dorsiflexion ROM with imposed FFS may provide more time to increase the dorsiflexion angular velocity ineccentric plantarflexors action.Alongwith the 

 increased ankle plantarflexor moment, a faster dorsiflexion velocity would explain the greater peak negative ankle plantarflexor power (i.e. product of moment and angular velocity), which is consistent with previous findings of greater eccentric plantarflexor action during imposed FFS (Hamill et al.,2014; Stearne et al., 2014; Williams et al., 2012).Injury to the Achilles tendon (e.g. tendinopathy) represents up to 18% of the total injuries in runners (McCrory et al., 1999). Thus, greater plantarflexor involvement presents the possibility of increased stresses to the ankle plantarflexor complex over time. Liebl,Willwacher,Hamill, and Bruggemann (2014)reported greater plantarflexor strength in habitual FFS compared to habitual RFS. Thus, it seems plausible that during a RFS to FFS transition, if the increased stresses placed on the plantarflexors are gradual, a positive chronic adaptation for strengthening of the plantarflexor may serve as a protective mechanism to tissue damage (e.g. Achilles tendon).

 Although the ankle plantarflexors appear to be more involved in FFS during stance, RFS produces 

 aDifferent than 4.47 m \mathsf{s}^{-1} within foot strike.

 a much greater dorsiflexor moment immediately following foot contact compared to imposed FFS (Table I; Figure 1). This early stance moment quantifies the net eccentric action of the dorsiflexors as the ankle plantarflexes to lower the foot to the ground immediately following heel strike (Perry, 1992).Many patients with chronic exertional compartment syndrome (CECS; lower leg pain) are distance runners (Detmer, Sharpe,Sufit,& Girdley,1985;Shah,Miller,&Kuhn,2004).Theeccentricaction of ankle dorsiflexors appears to be related to the onset of chronic anterior compartment syndrome (Tweed & Barnes, 2008) as greater intramuscular pressure of the tibialis anterior have been reported with excessive eccentric actions of the ankle (Friden, Sfakianos, & Hargens, 1986). Reduced eccentric dorsiflexor involvement during imposed FFS in runners seems to explain reductions in anterior compartment pressures in patients with CECS following a 6-week imposed FFS pattern intervention (Diebal et al., 2012). Peak dorsiflexion showed a foot strike and speed interaction. Post-hoc analyses showed that RFS generally produced greater ankle dorsiflexion compared to imposed FFS at both speeds but that the difference between strike patterns was not as large at the faster compared to the slow speed.


 As expected, habitual RFS showed greater peak knee extensor moment(Figure 2) andnegative power compared to imposed FFS similar to previously reported findings (Hamill et al., 2014;Stearne et al.,2014;Williams etal.,2012).In addition, a reduction in knee flexion ROM was also found in imposed FFS compared to habitual RFS (21.0%). The greater knee flexion ROM along with the unchanged peak knee flexion between strike patterns suggest a more extended knee at foot contact in RFS compared to imposed 

 FFS. Although greater knee extensor involvement in RFS running may increase axial contact forces within the knee due to increased muscle force contribution, previous research did not find differences in estimated axial knee contact forces between habitual RFS and imposed FFS using musculoskeletal modelling (Rooney & Derrick, 2013). Kulmala et al.(2013) did however report lower estimated patellofemoral contact force in habitual RFS compared to habitual FFS during running as a result of a lower knee extensor moment. Thus, although an acute transition to FFS in habitual RFS runners may be harmful to the plantarflexors due to greater peak plantarflexor moment and force production (Rooney & Derrick, 2013),this transition may be acutely beneficial for habitual RFS to evade on-going knee symptoms while a treatment or intervention is completed.


 Finally, all variables but peak dorsiflexor moment (nearly absent during FFS) showed a speed main effect. All kinetic variables and most kinematic variables increased with speed while knee flexion ROM was reduced at the faster speed. The increased magnitudes of kinetic variables with a faster speed were expected since GRFs increase with speed (De Wit, De Clercq,& Aerts,200O)and it is well established that peak joint ankle and knee moments increase with faster running speeds Arampatzis et al., 1999;de David et al., 2015; Petersen et al., 2014). Reduced knee flexion ROM with increased running speed has also been previously reported (Bishop, Fiolkowski, Conrad, Brunt,&Horodyski,2006).


 The current study focused on an acute strike pattern modification from habitual RFS to imposed FFS at two different common running speeds in competitive male runners in a non-fatigued state.It is possible that peripheral and/or central fatigue 

 during longer training runs, or training cycles, could alter neuromuscular control of lower extremity joints.Such fatigue effects could alter their kinematic and kinetic behaviour in early stance between foot strike patterns and ultimately, altering potential internal stresses placed on the joints. Additionally, runners wore their running shoes during testing procedures,which could introduce variability in our results.However, the current within-subject design addresses this limitation as in this design, the participants serve as their own control and thus, variability between subjects is no longer a factor (Vincent & Weir.2012). This study examined only competitive male runners who ran an average of 66.9 km per week (i.e. higher mileage). Our results may not be generaliz able to female runners and to runners who are less trained.

At TLC Columbus, there is always a reason to celebrate--healing and recovery,nursings giving high quality care, another day of life.


 The spring holds many special occasions.We welcome friends, family, and volunteers to share in the fun with us. Stop by the front desk for your copy of the monthly activities calendar.


 On Valentine's Day,TLC Palestine hosted a sweet party where residents enjoyed refreshments with their loved ones. question: What are the purpose of this presentation?

prediction.answer: To ensure spacecraft safety during re-entry, inspect and assess Thermal Protection System (TPS) for damage from orbital debris using robotic systems, high-resolution imaging, and focused inspections. If damage is found, repair or declare safe; if none, confirm re-entry readiness.

retrieved_documents.content:
Use spacecraft-to-spacecraft photography, robotics, a free-flyer, or surface crawler to systematically image the entire external surface of the reentry spacecraft 

 For high probability of detection (PoD), image the surface such that at least 4 resolution elements (resels) bridge the critical dimension of the smallest critical-sized damage.

 Perform a coverage analysis to ensure the entire surface was observed 

 Screen the survey imagery 

 Use redundant, independent teams to compare inspection imagery with baseline images of the same surface 


 Enhance process with automatic feature detection as available 

 Post all anomalous features or "Regions of Interest" (ROls) to a web-based log for disposition by a Damage Assessment Team (DAT)


 Disposition Regions of Interest and determine need for Focused Inspection 

 •If no Focused Inspection needed, declare the spacecraft safe to re-enter

In Space NDl Workshop - 29 February, 2012NASA Johnson Space Center 

 KX – Hypervelocity Impact Technology Eric L. Christiansen 

 KX – Image Science and Analysis Mike Rollins 

 Provide background on micro-meteoroid & orbital debris (MMOD) environment and risk Describe external inspection needs for re-entry TPS 

 Orbital Debris (OD) environment models 

 Orbital Debris environment (ORDEM2000): 1-17 km/s 

 Debris flux increases with increasing altitude up to about 1500km altitude 

 Debris is not a major factor above GEO altitude (35786km)

 Debris environment subject to change (ORDEM 3.0 release pending)

 Normalized Orbital Debris Flux by Year at ISS Altitude For 
Threat Particle Sizes > 0.3cm 
(Normalized to 2006 flux)

Note OD Risk is proportional to Flux

ISS Robotic Based Imagers – TPS Damage Detection (0.25" MPCV*TPS-type entry hole detection)


  >99% Probability of Detection (black or white tiles), assuming up to 3redundant/independent screening teams.


 Robotic trajectories not defined, so no timeline for above PoD for fullsurface inspection 


 ISS Robotic Based Imagers - Measurement:

 Transverse measurement accuracy (w.r.t. line of sight): ~0.07"

 Thermal Protection System (TPS) Tile cavity depth measurement accuracy: ~0.2" (Desired ~0.04” or 1 mm). Sensitive to entry hole width and ability to illuminate internal cavity.


 ISS Visiting Vehicles – Fly Around Imagery, 600' Range (example)

 -Transverse measurement accuracy (D3X, 105 mm lens) ~0.6"

 Corresponding damage detection resolution ~2.25"

 exo-LEO Reentry Vehicle TPS Inspection Capability TBD 


 *MPCV spacecraft not currently baselined for ISS missions (strategic backup only)
Enabling Technologies for articulating and mobile robots are being monitored by NASA personnel such as George Studor and the Image Science and Analysis Group

question: What is the mean time to first decision according to the editor's comment?

retrieved_documents.content:
By now, you will have noticed that we have changed to the new version, V3, of Manuscript Central. There is a new URL which is http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/bjui Those of you who have tried to log onto the system using the old URL will automatically have been redirected to the new link.I am delighted with this new version of Manuscript Central and I very much hope that all of you who have used it feel likewise. The new version is quicker, more user-friendly and more appealing to the user. This in turn will further accelerate our review process,enabling us to get a decision to you, the authors, as soon as possible.I would like to remind you that our mean time to first decision is already quite speedy at 22.5 days.It is probably apposite also to remind readers that our rejection rate is 75%, and even higher in the Investigative Urology section.Once again I would like to encourage readers and users of the system to write to us with their comments. all of which will be taken onboard to help us produce an even better product.


 We are publishing several mini-reviews at present, but these are commissioned either by myself or by one of the Associate Editors. I apologise if prospective authors send in minireviews independent of this and find that they are rejected.I would like to point out the importance of the Comments section of 

 the Journal. This is very widely read, and generates much interest and discussion. The topics are at the 'cutting edge' and can be as provocative as the author wishes. They should not exceed 1000 words, with no more than 10references, and can be submitted online without being commissioned.


 Iam often asked to talk to residents about producing a paper which is likely to succeed in being accepted for publication.I very much enjoy doing this, but often forget to mention one item which came to mind on scanning the pages in this issue of the journal. The importance of colourful illustrations cannot be overstated; they help to demonstrate so much of what the author is trying to describe in words in an easy and rapidly understandable way.I would like to encourage authors to use colour illustrations,as this makes the paper more understandable and more attractive. It does mean a little extra trouble, but please remember that they are reproduced in the BJU International free of charge to the Author.

Get your diploma in sock knitting with this pattern specifically designed for a beginning Sock-aholic. This simple toe-up sock allows the knitter to tailor the width and length to the wearer's foot size for a perfect no-fuss fit. Stepby-step tutorials are included to help you with the techniques used, and video support is available for this project,too! This pattern is written for the Two Circular Needle method, so grab a pair and get knitting!

 YARN: Knitters Brewing Co: sock-aholicor sock-aholic Sweet Fingering Weight Yarn,One skein 

 SIZING: Sock width and length is established by the knitter.

 NEEDLES: Two 24" Circular Needles in US Size 0, 1, or 2 as needed to achieve the correct gauge.

 HORIZONTAL GAUGE:32..-${\sf s t s}=4$ .in Stockinette Stitch (8 sts per inch)

 ADDITIONAL SUPPLIES NEEDED: Stitch marker, blunt-tipped tapestry needle 

 k knit 

k2tog knit two together (a right-leaning decrease). Insert RH needle into the front of the next 2 sts 
from left to right, and knit the two sts together as one stitch.

LH left-hand 

LLI left lifted increase. Knit 1. With LH needle pick up the left side loop of the st below the 
st just worked from the back, and then knit that loop through the back.
p purl 

and purl the two sts together as one stitch.

RH right-hand 

RLI right lifted increase. Knit into the right leg of the st below the next st on the LH needle,
and then knit the next st.

sl slip stitch from LH to RH needle (purlwise unless noted differently)
ssk slip slip knit (a left-leaning decrease). Slip one stitch as if to knit it (but don't). Slip the next st 
in the same way. Insert LH needle into the front of these two sts from left to right, and knit the 
two sts together as one.

ssp slip slip purl. Slip one stitch as if to knit it (but don't). Slip the next st in the same way.
Return the two sts to the LH needle in their new orientation. Insert RH needle into the 
back of these two sts from left to right, and purl the two sts together as one.

retrieved_documents.content:
By Orna Sheena 

 Throughout history, we, the Jewish people, have experienced many ups and downs and trials that tested the resiliency that sustained us. We have fought for our survival. We have safeguarded our heritage and the wisdom of the Torah, which has been passed down from generation to generation. We have succeeded when we pulled together as a united community.

 At TBT, we teach our students about the challenging times in Jewish history and focus on our ability to turn tragedy into triumph. During the period of Passover, we learned about the transition from slavery to freedom. And in recent weeks,we demonstrated the transition from sadness -remembering the Holocaust, as well as the fallen heroes of Israel -- into a joyous celebration of Yom Ha'Atzmaut, Israel's Independence Day.

 Wecommemorated Yom HaShoah in a solemn candle-lighting ceremony with readings about the Holocaust. And on Yom HaZikaron, Israel's Remembrance Day, we remembered the thousands who lost their lives defending Israel, as well as those who were victims of terror.


 To mark Israel's 65th Independence Day, students discussed Israel's miracle of survival, its growth,and achievements. They watched videos which showcased Israel's innovation and entrepreneurship in so many spheres -- culture, technology, business,medicine, and far more. In spite of the overwhelming challenges such as wars and terror attacks that this tiny country experiences on a daily basis, its people demonstrate remarkable resilience and courage.

 To make Israel's birthday celebration extra sweet, our students decorated sponge cakes with white frosting and blue sprinkles to represent the flag of Israel, and of course they enjoyed eating them.

 Bored with what you've been cooking?Need New Ideas?

 Price: $18.00 per copyChecks made out to: TBT Sisterhood Available for Purchase in Temple Offi  ce 

 Also, in keeping with Israel's security challenges,our students were put through military exercises by our favorite Israeli IDF soldier Amit -- an all-time favorite activity.


 We continue to learn about Israel and important Biblical characters through an interactive WebQuest,an inquiry-oriented project in which the students gather information about their personalities from online resources. In Special Activities, Mrs. Levine has them creating and dressing their Biblical characters. Once the project is complete, the children will present their findings to the other classes and trace their character's journey in Israel and neighboring countries using the large Israel fl oor map.


 During the upcoming month, as we celebrate Lag B'Omer, Yom Yerushalayim (Jerusalem Day),and Shavuot, we are again reminded that our beliefs,traditions, and heritage give us the determination and courage to secure our future.

In Fig.3 a simplified schematic of the implemented "wheel amplifier" is shown. The reset switches on C3 and C2 are not shown for the sake of simplicity. In the figure an individual unit is shown (referenced as unit 1). The step voltage signal Vst provided by the preamplifier is converted into a current by means of a resettable transconductor (OPA1,R,C),note that one per each unit is used. The trapezoidal weight functions are then constructed by properly switching the current provided by the transconductor by means of T1. First the current is integrated over Ci (position a of T1), then it is flown to ground (position b), eventually it is integrated over C3(position c) whereas OPA4 acts as an inverting amplifier. It may be shown (see [6]) that by sequencing the operations in such a way a trapezoidal weight function results, provided that the two integration times are equal and $C2=C3$ .After such switching sequence is accomplished the output voltages are sampled through switches T3,which actually implement the 

 rotating switch of Fig.1. In order to guarantee a corect operation all the units should be identical. The uniformity of the gains of the units is related to technological parameters (accurateness in the ratio of on-chip resistor and capacitor values). With the used technology the expected disomogeneites are in the subpercent range, which is compatible with high resolution X-ray spectroscopy (up to a few tens of keV), where the spectral resolutions are typically in the some-percent range.

Programm und Termine der VBI-Seminarreihe zum Einsatz der Fidic-Vertragsbedingungen für 2016 liegen vor. Fidic ist der internationale Dachverband der beratenden Ingenieure. Die Reihe bietet wertvolle Einstiegshilfen wie auch vertiefende Einsichten für alle Ingenieure,Architekten und Berater, die ihre Leistungen auch in internationalen Projekten anbieten. Die Fidic-Vertragsbedingungen stellen eine vertragliche Grundlage für internationale Projekte dar, die zunehmend Anwendung finden.

 Die VBI-Seminarreihe setzt sich aus einzeln buchbaren Modulen zusammen.Sie startet am 1. April 2016 mit einem einführenden Basis-Kurs. Am 22. und 23. April 2016 folgt der zweitagige Hauptkurs, der in die Grundlagen der Arbeit mit dem ,FIDIC Red Book" und "Yellow Book" einführt. Die vertiefenden Kurse finden ab September im Herbst statt,Die Seminarreihe bietet 

 auch die Möglichkeit einer zertifizierten Ausbildung zum Dispute Adjudicator nach Fidic. Bei Vorliegen weiterer Voraussetzungen berechtigt diese zur Aufnahme in die VBI-geführte Liste der deutschsprachigen ,FIDIC Dispute Adjudicators": www.vbi.de/infopooll/dispute-adjudication/


 In den Seminaren erhalten die Teilnehmer Informationen aus erster Hand:Dipl.-Ing. Wirt. Ing. Axel-Volkmar Jaeger war als Vorsitzender des FIDIC Contracts Committee unmittelbar an der Erstellung der aktuellen Vertragswerke beteiligt.Ihm zur Seite steht Rechtsanwalt Dr. Sebastian Hk, der die zentralen Fidic-Bücher übersetzt und erläutert.Die beiden Experten stellen kompetent Systematik, Zusammenhänge und Anwendung der Bücher vor.

事件：23H1实现营业收入35.3亿元，同比+16.6%；归母净利润9.2亿元，同比+10.1%，23Q2公司营收15.08亿元，同比+32%；归母净利润3.50亿元，同比+15%，均符合预期。


 收入端快速增长，指标合理。公司综合把控渠道回款/库存/价格，上半年实现时间过半任务过半。Q2收入同比+32%，其中中高档酒10.9亿同比+31%，预计品味舍得保持平均增速，舍之道增速表现较优，老酒有所承压：低档酒2.8亿/同比+34%，增速较快，也符合01末沱牌提价前后整体促回款（我们预计沱牌回款过半，部分地区完成任务)。Q2销售收现17.8亿元/同比+36%，保持稳定增长且高于收入增速；截至Q2末公司有合同负债5.1亿，同比+18%。


 1投入&并表影响下，净利率有所波动.Q2销售净利率23.4%/同比-3.8pct，其中Q2毛利率71.9%/同比-1.9pct，主要系Q2夜郎古并表表现，我们预计主业毛利率基本持平（中低端产品费用投入相对更小)；税金率15.8%/同比+2.4pct，主要系房产和酒生产增加所致（全年预计相对稳定)，销售费用及费用率同比+29%/-0.4pct，主要系并表后部分费用的投入增加，总体保持相对稳定区间；管理费用及费用率同比+48%/+1.2pct，主要系股权激励费用分摊及新产品项目研发投入增加所致。

 1增加费用投放，多产品多场景抓份额。公司2023年规划清晰，持续高度重视品味舍得在宴席的推广，藏品十年更新尚未正式铺开，预计下半年有所贡献；上半年原优势区域稳定，部分新市场通过会战/招商拓展，下半年将继续大力发展优质经销商。7-8月公司持续做强动销去库存，新市场的投入有所增加，预计旺季公司将继续加大终端费用的投入，商务宴请场景相对有压力，通过多场景/多产品线持续抢占市场份额，全年快速增长仍可期。


 盈利预测与投资评级：公司拟以自有资金（1.85-3.7亿元区间）回购股份以用于员工持股计划，长期看在股权激励/回购计划加持、精准市场把控下公司量先价稳。我们调整2023-2025年归母净利润为19.96、25.06、32.46亿元（前值为21、27、33亿元），分别同比+18%、26%、30%（前值分别为+26%、+27%、+22%)，当前市值对应2023-2025年PE分别为23、18、14倍，维持“买入”评级。

retrieved_documents.content:
The Graveyard Book by Neil Gaiman (1 edition)

 Purpose: Theuroirgitnatdtiiwiticaulary words in a fun way. The students are exposed to the words and definitions twice throughout the activity,once when choosing the words they want on their bingo cards and once when the teacher calls the definition out loud.


 Context: The Gravyard Book haalotofbigwordsthatcan beconfusingfor students,itis important to give the students as many opportunities to engage with the vocabulary. Use this activity after you have already introduced the vocabulary words to the students, but before you have finished the novel.


 Step 1: Give each student abingocard (if you would like to re-use thecards,have them laminated and give the students erasable markers to write on them with). Read the vocabulary words with the definitions and have the students fill in the blanks on their bingo card with the vocabulary words.


 Step 2: After the cards are prepared read only the definition out loud. It will be the students'responsibility to find the corresponding vocabulary word. The students will then place some kind of token on their cards to signify that they found the matching word.

 Step3: Oncetudentets bgohave themcall ut which words theyhavecovered on their card. Verify that the words they have are correct. If they have words that are incorrect figure out where they went wrong and then continue to call out vocabulary definitions.

 Assessment: As the students are marking their bingo cards, observe the students. Students should be able to successfully find the correct word on their card to correspond with the given definition. It will be easy to identify which words students are struggling with as you observe the class. It will also be apparent if students read off incorrect word choices from their bingo card.This will help in to determine which words students need to practice more.

 Kara Allen, BYU, 2009
