Table 1 gives details of the results from the ICUs in the study. The John Radcliffe Hospital Adult ICU is a general adult ICU admitting secondary and tertiary referrals, and the Neurosciences ICU admits patients with neurological or neurosurgical problems and patients after head and neck surgery. The Churchill Hospital Adult ICU is in a hospital without an Emergency Department and admits patients after elective surgery as well as patients from specialist medical and surgical wards. The Royal Berkshire and Wycombe General Hospital ICUs are large-district general hospital ICUs.

 Table 2 gives the sound levels averaged for each site over the whole 24-hour period recorded both at the central station and adjacent to the patient. The averaged time × sound-level plots for all ICUs for both recording positions are shown in Figures l and 2. All ICUs recorded $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A e q}}$ levels above 45 dBA at all times and between 52 and 59 dBA for more than 50% of the time.

 Figure 3 gives the cumulative frequency plot of the $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A p e a k}}$ :recorded minute-by-minute in each ICU adjacent 

 adB, decibel. Sound levels averaged over 24 hours for each site. At the John Radcliffe Hospital, the Royal Berkshire Hospital and Wycombe General Hospital, two recordings were measured: on a weekday day and night and on a weekend day and night. Only one recording was taken at the Neurosciences ICU and the Churchill Hospital ICU in Oxford.


 to the patient. In more than 50% of the minutes sampled,$\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A p e a k}}$ occurred between 79.0 dBA and 84.6dBA. During every minute sampled, there was a $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A p e a k}}$ :in excess of 60 dBA. The highest $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A p e a k}}$ recorded was 127.9 dBA.


 Figure 4 shows the average number of minutes per hour across all sites when $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A p e a k}}$ above both 85 dBA and 100 dBA were recorded next to the patient. There is a clear diurnal variation that correlates with the $\mathrm{L_{A e q}}$ T plots of Figures 1 and 2.


 Figure 5 shows the frequency components of the noise recorded adjacent to a patient in the adult ICU at the John Radcliffe Hospital averaged for an hour at the quietest time (4:00 AM to 5:00 AM) and during working hours (4:00 PM to 5:00 PM). The frequency scale is logarithmic in third-octave frequency bands.

 Recordings were also taken at midmorning in an empty side room at the John Radcliffe Hospital Adult ICU with the door closed and the recording equipment placed where the patient's head would normally be situated. With all equipment in the room switched off, the $\mathrm{L_{A e q}}$ was 34.1 dBA, with increases (1) to 43.5 dBA when the ventilator was running with a test lung; (2) to 47.2or 51.2 dBA when the ventilator sounded a low- or 

 high-levelalert,respectively;(3)to53.0 dBAwith the suction unit turned on; (4) to 59.2 dBA when the monitor sounded a high-level alert; and (5) to 63.3 dBA when the syringe pumps were alerted.


 Some frequency components of the alarm noise were identified using one-third octave frequency plots. The physiological monitors showed frequency peaks in the 1.6- to 3.15-kHz bandsfor the first-level alarm and 2.5to 3.15-kHz peaks for the second-level (more urgent)alarm. The infusion pump alarms registered frequency peaks in the 800-Hz to 1-kHz range. The ventilator alarms contained a broad spread of frequencies and could not easily be distinguished from other sounds.

The chart is a cumulative frequency plot showing peak sound levels (LApk) measured at one-minute intervals throughout the day by a recording device positioned adjacent to the patient, in decibels (dB). The vertical axis represents cumulative frequency (%) and the horizontal axis represents LApk (dB). The plot includes two distinct lines: one for hospital weekend and one for hospital weekday, each represented by a different line style and legend. The data indicates that as LApk increases, the cumulative frequency also increases, showing a higher proportion of measurements at higher sound levels. The chart visually compares the distribution of sound levels between weekend and weekday hospital settings, with the weekend line generally positioned slightly higher than the weekday line, suggesting higher sound levels on weekends.

 行业：综合及概念/医疗美容/医美相关服务/医美服务

 综合性企业/综合企业

 版权有问题？点此投诉
隆鼻行业属于医美服务，需要客户承担一定的医疗风险是典型的特征。由于包含手术类和非手术类项目，隆鼻的服务定价较为宽泛，取决于植入材料的成本和操作方式的难易程度。不同价位的隆鼻项目对应不同的细分客群，每一细分市场的竞争激烈程度相对有限。同时，新进入者需要采购合规的医疗器械及药品、聘请专业医生及建设品牌，巨大的资金需求使得准入门槛提高。

隆鼻行业的发展以隆鼻手术的发展为重要线索。从萌芽期的石蜡植入到启动期的固体硅胶，再到高速发展期的假体与自体组织结合材料，最后到成熟期的手术类与非手术类隆鼻项目并行，鼻部的植入物朝着“更安全、更拟真”的方向发展。


 萌芽期·1930-01-01~1948-12-31

 20世纪30年代，隆鼻美容手术已经在上海、北京等地出现，大部分均通过注射石蜡隆鼻。

 隆鼻行业的萌芽以隆鼻假体手术的兴起为代表，早期使用的石蜡会使身体出现排斥反应且会引起并发症，逐渐被淘汰。

中国隆鼻行业的上游厂商主要提供植入物、注射物、药物、护肤品和其他医用耗材，以华东医药和华熙生物为代表；中游企业主要是私立医美机构，以艺星医美和瑞丽医美为代表；下游参与者主要是医美获客平台和终端消费者，医美获客平台以新氧为代表。


 从上游的角度看，植入物、注射物等耗用品占据中游企业较多的销售成本，上游产品的价格波动对产业链产生的影响较大，并且获得国际品牌授权的国内经销商能获取较高的毛利；从中游的角度看，提供消费性医疗服务的非公立机构是主要参与者，隆鼻项目服务并非同质化，面向不同细分客群的医美机构竞争关系较弱；从下游的角度看，消费者对医美的风险识别意识增强驱动线上医美获客平台的发展，而终端消费者的需求具有区域性特点。

question: When did M. Christopher Brown II begin his role as AERA Director of Social Justice and Professional Development?

retrieved_documents.content:
AERA is pleased to announce the appointment of M. Christopher Brown II as AERA Director of Social Justice and Professional Development, effective January 3, 2005. Brown has set up his office at AERA headquarters and is already moving important programmatic work ahead.


 Before assuming his post at AERA, Brown served as executive director and chief research scientist of the Frederick D. Patterson Research Institute at the United Negro College Fund. He is on a leave of absence from The Pennsylvania State University, where he is an associate professor of education and senior research associate in the Center for the Study of Higher Education. He also has taught at the Uni

 versity of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign. Known for his studies of historically Black colleges, educational equity, and institutional culture, he is the author or co-author of more than 0 journal articles, book chapters, monographs, and publications related to education and society. His books include The Quest to Define Collegiate Desegregation: Black Colleges, Title VI Compliance and Post-Adams Litigation (1999), Orga nization and Governance in Higher Education: An ASHE Reader (2000), and Black Sons to Mothers:Compliments, Critiques, and Challenges for Cultural Workers in Education (2000, with coauthor James Earl Davis). He also has two books in press—Black Colleges: New Perspectives on Policy and Praxis (with coauthor Kassie Freeman) and The Politics of Curricular Change: Race, Hegemony and Power in Education (with coauthor Roderic Land).


 Brown's research and scholarly writing on higher education policy and administration merited him both the 2001 ASHE Promising Scholar/Early Career Award and the AERA Committee on Scholars of Color in Education 2002 Early Career Contribution Award. He has also been an AERA–Spencer Foundation Fellowship recipient. An active AERA member since 1999, he is a member and chair-elect of the Government Relations Committee and served on the Minority Fellowship Committee from 2000 to 2003.

 Felice Levine, Executive Director, notes that Brown "will play an important role in advancing social justice in the field of education research, and will also work to promote social justice considerations in the ac tivities and initiatives of AERA." One of Brown's key responsibilities is capacity building in education research through training and professional development, including directing the AERA-IES Postdoctoral Training Program and the Minority Fellowship Program.

INTERC0OL NFE is chemically engineered using organic and inorganic inhibitors for corrosion protection in a ethylene glycol base and used as an Industrial Heat Transfer Fluid. The chemistry employed will effectively protect your industrial equipment whether constructed of single or mixed alloys. The industrial corrosion control inhibitors included have been specially formulated to extend the service life of the coolant. Therefore, you will receive a higher level of reserve alkalinity, superior performance, and less maintenance requirements through reinhibiting. These attributes improve your bottom line cost.

 INTERCOOLOP-101 is added initially and for remediation purposes when required. INTERC0OL inhibitors, when added to ethylene glycol in the prescribed amount will protect such metals as Brass, Copper, Copper alloys, Steel, and Cast iron. INTERCOOL inhibitors have successfully completed the336-hour ASTM D1384 corrosion test. A certificate of assurance is available upon request.

 INTERCOOL NFE can be purchased in its concentrated form or preblended with deionized water to meet your specification for boiling, freeze and/or burst protection.

question: What is the title?
answer: ​​SUCCESSFUL REPAIR OF ESOPHAGEAL INJURY USING ELASTIN BASED BIOMATERIAL PATCH​

prediction.answer: SUCCESSFUL REPAIR OF ESOPHAGEAL INJURY USING ELASTIN BASED BIOMATERIAL PATCH

retrieved_documents.content:
SUCCESSFUL REPAIR OF ESOPHAGEAL INJURY USING ELASTIN BASED BIOMATERIAL PATCH Michio Kajitani MD,PhD,Yasmin Wadia MD,Monica T.Hinds PhD Jeffrey Teach RN, Kim Swartz MD, Kenton G. Gregory MD Oregon Medical Laser Center, 9205 SW Barnes Rd. Portland, OR 97225

 Purpose: An esophageal injury with significant tissue loss is very difficult to repair primarily,and has high morbidity and mortality with stricture and leak. We conducted in vivo study to test our elstin based biomaterial patch composed of elastin and collagen to repair the large tissue defect in the esophagus.


 Method: The elastin based biomaterial was made from porcine aorta,by decellulization, and sterilization. Collagen coating on the adventitia was preserved to retain mechanical strength and to enhance cellular ingrowth. Ten domestic pigs underwent right thoracotomy. A 2cm circular defect was made on the distal esophagus,excising half of its circumference,and was repaired using the elastin based biomaterial patch and running absorbable sutures. Soon after closure and extubation, the animals were allowed to resume oral feeding. They were followed for clinica! status, weight gain, and endoscopic studies. They were electively sacrificed after six weeks to four months.


 Results: All ten animals survived long term, giving success rate of 100%(10/10). One animal developed tension pneumothorax early after surgery,which was properly treated. No other complication occurred. They all resumed oral feeding soon after the surgery, and gained weight. No infection was encountered. Endoscopic studies showed complete mucosal coverage by six weeks. There was minimal to no anatomical or functional stricture at the repair site. Excised specimens after sacrifice showed complete mucosal coverage of the repair site,with regeneration of the all three layers.


 Conclusion: Our Elastin based biomaterial patch can be used safely and reliably for repair of esophageal injury with significant tissue loss.Complete regeneration of the esophageal wall was observed when repaired immediately after the injury in our experiment.

question: What is the title?
answer: Foreign Protein Expression Using Plant Cell Suspension and Hairy Root Cultures

prediction.answer: Foreign Protein Expression Using Plant Cell Suspension and Hairy Root Cultures

retrieved_documents.content: As indicated by the protein accumulation levels in Table 2.1, it is currently possible using plant tissue culture to achieve moderate levels of foreign protein expression in some systems. To take advantage of the cost benefits associated with recovering products from the culture medium rather than from homogenized biomass, expression systems for protein secretion have also been developed. Yet, relative to the production levels attained in animal cell cultures, foreign protein concentrations in plant cultures are typically very low. This is a major hurdle preventing plant systems being utilized more widely for commercial protein production. Therefore, a key research objective has been to increase the accumulation of active foreign proteins in plant tissue culture. Although the reasons for the low yields in plant systems are not yet fully understood, two different approaches have been taken to increase product levels in vitro. These are: (i) to increase the level of gene expression in the cells by altering the transgene constructs and methods of expression, and (ii) to increase the retention and stability of foreign protein in the cultures after the protein is produced. Ef forts have also been made to enhance the availability of product in the culture medium to facilitate subsequent recovery and purification.

In this review, we focus on the use of plant tissue culture to produce foreign proteins that have direct commercial or medical applications. The development of large-scale plant tissue culture systems for the production of biopharmaceutical proteins requires efficient, high-level expression of stable, biologically active products.To minimize the cost of protein recovery and purification, it is preferable that the expression system releases the product in a form that can be harvested from the culture medium. In addition, the relevant bioprocessing issues associated with bioreactor culture of plant cells and tissues must be addressed.


 Extensive research has been carried out into the molecular aspects of foreign protein production in whole plants to enhance the yield, quality and stability of the product and to facilitate protein separation and purification from the biomass [3, 6, 9].In contrast, comparatively little research has been undertaken to investigate the 

 specific issues associated with producing foreign proteins in plant tissue culture.Many of the problems that need to be addressed, such as low protein yields, are similar in vivo and in vitro. However, the differences between these systems means that different solutions may be required.


 Some developments that have proven useful for enhancing foreign protein yields in whole plants, such as chloroplast and organ-specific expression systems, have lit tle or no practical application in tissue culture. On the other hand, although not yet demonstrated in vitro, the use of viral vectors to increase protein production, and novel approaches for simplifying product purification such as targeting of proteins to oil bodies, may have significant implications for the production of foreign proteins in tissue culture systems.


 Foreign proteins that have been produced using plant tissue culture are listed in Table 2.1. Several of these proteins, e.g. antibodies,interleukins, erythropoietin, hu man granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor (hGM-CSF) and hepatitis B antigen, have pharmaceutical or therapeutic uses and would be suitable for further commercial development if the production levels could be increased. However, to date, few plant cell or organ cultures have been shown to accumulate or secrete for eign proteins at concentrations sufficient for commercial viability.

 As indicated in Table 2.1, tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) has been the host species in most studies of foreign protein expression in plant tissue culture. Rice (Oryza sativa)has also been used by several groups. Accumulation of hGM-CSF was found to be significantly higher in rice suspensions with an inducible promoter than in tobacco suspensions with constitutive transgene expression [10]. The predominance of tobacco-based expression systems in tissue culture studies differs from the situation with whole plants, where advantages associated with producing foreign proteins in edible species or in storage organs such as seeds have resulted in a variety of plant species being transformed.


 Most research into in vitro foreign protein production has been undertaken using cell suspensions. However, other forms of plant tissue culture such as hairy roots and shooty teratomas have also been tested in a number of studies (Table 2.1). The characteristics of different types of plant tissue culture and their utility for large-scale foreign protein production are outlined in the following sections.

Plant cell suspensions comprise small clumps of dedifferentiated plant cells in li quid nutrient medium. Dedifferentiation of the cells occurs under the influence of plant growth regulators, which must be provided in the medium to promote rapid growth and maintain the culture morphology. Transgenic cell suspensions can be developed from callus initiated using explants from transformed plants; alternatively,wild-type suspensions may be transformed directly using Agrobacterium tumefaciensmediated transfection or biolistic delivery of plasmid DNA into the cells (Table 2.1).

 Plant suspensions are being used to produce an increassing number of foreign proteins. These include complete antibodies, antibody fragments, hGM-CSF, interleu

 kin-2, interleukin-4 and interleukin-12, erythropoietin, hepatitis B surface antigen,α1-antitrypsin, human lysozyme and carrot invertase. Although, in some instances,stable production of foreign proteins has been found to occur over extended periods [11-15], suspension cultures are subject to various types of genetic instability through the effects of somaclonal variation [16]. Significant reductions in the yield of foreign proteins over time, possibly caused by genetic instability, have been reported in plant cell suspensions [12–14, 17]. In some cases, cell lines with stable production characteristics were isolated by screening and selection from a large number of cultures [12–14].

question: What is the title?
answer: Connected Choices: How the Internet is Challenging Sovereign Decisions

prediction.answer: Connected Choices: How the Internet is Challenging Sovereign Decisions

retrieved_documents.content:
ourinternet.org 

 PAPER SERIES: NO. 11APRIL 2015

The Global Commission on Internet Governance was established in January 2014 to articulate and advance a strategic vision for the future of Internet governance. The two-year project conducts and supports independent research on Internet-related dimensions of global public policy, culminating in an official commission report that will articulate concrete policy recommendations for the future of Internet governance. These recommendations will address concerns about the stability, interoperability,security and resilience of the Internet ecosystem.

 Launched by two independent global think tanks,the Centre for International Governance Innovation (CIGI) and Chatham House, the Global Commission on Internet Governance will help educate the wider public on the most effective ways to promote Internet access,while simultaneously championing the principles of freedom of expression and the free flow of ideas over the Internet.


 The Global Commission on Internet Governance will focus on four key themes:

 enhancing  governance  legitimacy  — including regulatory approaches and standards;

 stimulating economic innovation and growth —including critical Internet resources, infrastructure and competition policy;

 ensuring human rights online — including establishing the principle of technological neutrality  for human rights, privacy and free expression; and 


 avoiding  systemic risk — including establishing norms regarding state conduct, cybercrime cooperation and non-proliferation,  confidencebuilding measures and disarmament issues.

 The goal  of the  Global  Commission on Internet Governance is two-fold.First, it will encourage globally inclusive public discussions on the future of Internet governance. Second, through its comprehensive policyoriented report,  and the subsequent  promotion of this  final report,  the Global Commission on Internet Governance will communicate its findings with senior stakeholders at key Internet governance events.

 www.ourinternet.org

Modern societies are in the middle of a strategic, multidimensional competition for money, power and control over all aspects of the Internet and the Internet economy.This paper discusses the increasing pace of discord and the competing interests that are unfolding in the current debate concerning the control and governance of the Internet and its infrastructure. Some countries are more prepared for and committed to winning tactical battles than others on the road to asserting themselves as an Internet power. Some are acutely aware of what is at stake; the question is whether they will be the master or the victim of these multi-layered power struggles as subtle and not-so-subtle connected choices are being made.Understanding this debate requires an appreciation of the entangled economic, technical, regulatory, political and social interests implicated by the Internet. Those states that are prepared for and understand the many facets the Internet presents will likely end up on top.

question: What is the title of the hymn shown in the image?

retrieved_documents.content:
1. Could we explain the night in which Jesus was in the Garden praying as a night where wings of darkness hovered over the valley (Night, with ebon pinion, Brooded o'er the vale)? Yes.

 Remember that the Lord's Supper was instituted at night prior to the prayer in the Garden (I Corinthians 11:23; cf. Matthew 26:26ff.).


 By way of implication, it was a late night (Matthew 26:31; 34; 40-41; 43; 45).

 Figuratively, it was the night in which our Lord was betrayed, thus one could say darkness hovered that night in a spiritual sense (Luke 22:46-53). Darkness was spreading this night.


 2. Could we say that the night in which Jesus was praying was silent with exception to the normal noises of that night's wind? Yes.


 By way of implication there would not have been much activity at a sleeping hour.Jesus also liked to pray in solitude, thus one could say the implication of being alone would leave only the noise of a night wind (Matthew 14:23 and Luke 6:12; cf.Matthew 6:6).


 3. Could Christ be explained as a Man of Sorrows in tears and sweat as blood on this night? Yes (Matthew 26:37, Mark 14:32-34, and Luke 22:44).


 4.Was Jesus prostrate in the garden this night raising His voice to His Father? Yes, Jesus was on the ground, stretched out, in submission to the Father (Mark 14:35).

 5. Was Jesus smitten for offenses which were not His own? Yes, (Isaiah 53:5, Romans 5:1521, Hebrews 4:14-15, and Hebrews 7:22-28).


 6. Was Christ meek, lowly, humbly bowed in prayer, and alone on this night in the garden because of our transgressions? Yes, (Matthew 11:28-30, Luke 22:41-42, and I Corinthians 15:3).


 7. Did He pray "Abba, Father, Father" asking for this cup of anguish to pass from Him? Yes,(Mark 14:36). Abba means "Father" and He did approach Him three times.

 8. Did Christ, as God's only begotten Son, accept the will of the Father in dying for our transgressions? Yes, (John 10:17-18 and Hebrews 5:8-9).


 9. Do you have any scriptural objections to singing this song in worship to God? No. *It should be noted that the third verse of this song has quotation marks around it. While the statements in that verse are true, this verse is not a direct quote of any passage in the New Testament.

Diagram (a) and (b) show similar configurations with slight variations in the aluminum section's shape, while diagram (c) presents a different structural arrangement with the aluminum section positioned below the SKTM 11A component. All diagrams include dimensional annotations (e.g., 0.00, 0.002 in) and a coordinate system with x, y, z axes. The top right corner of each diagram features the "ANSYS" logo, indicating the simulation software used. The caption below the image identifies the figures as "FIGURE 1. Deformation of Impact Attenuator: (a) Model 1, (b) Model 2, and (c) Model 3."

The image displays three simulation results from ANSYS software illustrating the deformation of an impact attenuator under load. Each panel (a, b, and c) corresponds to a different model (Model 1, Model 2, and Model 3, respectively) and shows a color-coded stress or displacement map on the attenuator's structure. The color bars indicate the magnitude of deformation or stress, with values ranging from 0.000000 mm to 0.000000 mm (or similar units) for displacement, and from 0.000000 MPa to 0.000000 MPa for stress. The models are shown in 3D, with the deformation exaggerated for visualization, and each includes coordinate axes and scale indicators. The figure is labeled as "FIGURE. 3 Deformation of Impact Attenuator: (a) Model 1, (b) Model 2, and (c) Model 3."

The line chart depicts the energy absorption over time for three different models, labeled as Model 1 (blue line), Model 2 (gray line), and Model 3 (yellow line). The horizontal axis represents time in seconds (S), ranging from 0 to 0.006, while the vertical axis represents energy (E) in scientific notation, ranging from 5E+12 to 4.5E+13. Model 3 (yellow) shows a sharp spike at the very beginning (near time 0), then drops to zero. Model 2 (gray) shows a steep increase starting around time 0.001, followed by a plateau. Model 1 (blue) remains near zero until approximately time 0.004, then exhibits a very rapid and steep increase, surpassing the other models.

retrieved_documents.content:
Frequency upconversion (UC) in lanthanide $\mathrm{(L n^{3+})}$ doped nanocrystals has been investigated intensively in the past decade due to their applications in solid-state blue-green lasers [1], three-dimensional color displays [2],solar cells [3], and especially fluorescent biolabels  [4-6]. Recently, white UC emission from $\mathtt{L n}^{3+}$ -doped nanocrystals has attracted increasing attention because of many potential applications [7-17]. Firstly, these nanocrystals can serve as lighting sources in nano-optical devices or as backlight sources.especially in three-dimensional backlighting for color displays.Secondly,the use of white UC nanocrystals as fluorescent biolabels can offer more simultaneous detection channels compared to UC or conventional biolabels, thereby facilitating probing of protein folding via the frequency resonant energy-transfer (ET) technique or allowing molecular fluorescence detection independent of the solution color [11]. Very recently, inspired by the report on white light simulation [16] in triply doped fluoride glass host, white UC emission under the excitation of a 980 nm laser diode (LD) has been achievedfrom $\mathrm{Yb^{3+}-Er^{3+}-Tm^{3+}}\quad\mathrm{or}\quad\mathrm{Yb^{3+}-Ho^{3+}-Tm^{3+}}$ doped glass ceramics containing $\mathtt{P b}_{1\mathrm{--}x}\mathsf{C d}_{x}\mathtt{F}_{2}\quad[7]$ or $\mathtt{Y F}_{3}$ nanocrystals [8,9],$\Upsilon_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanocrystals $[10,11]$ 1.$\mathrm{L u}_{3}\mathrm{{\sf{G a}}}_{5}\mathrm{{\sf{O}}}_{12}$ nanocrystals [12]],$\mathrm{L u}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ _nanocrystals [13],cubic $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanocrystals [14], dielectric 

 $\mathrm{{\tt{B a T i O}}}_{3}$ nanocrysals [1], and cuic $\mathtt{K G d F}_{4}$ nanocrystals [17]. However, there have been few investigations on white UC emission in hexagonal $\mathrm{N a Y F_{4}(\beta-N a Y F_{4})}$ nanostructures [18], which are identified as the most effi cient host materials for UC emission $[19-29]$ On the other hand, although the luminescent spectra of Ln3 doped nanocrystals vary little from host to host, the population densities of the donor and the acceptor states can be manipulated by precise control of the dopant concentration or pump-power densities as well as the size of nanocrystals, which influences the relative intensities of the different emission bands leading to changes in the color output from the $\mathrm{L n}^{3+}\mathrm{-d o p e d}\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanostructures [14,20,30,31]. For example, Wang et al. have presented an approach to fine-tune the UC emission color from visible to NIR under 980 nm excitation in cubic $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanoparticles by precisely controlling the combination of $\mathtt{L n}^{3+}$ ions and concentration [14].Schietinger et al. have reported that the UC properties of single $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanocrystals co-doped with $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}$ and $\bar{\mathtt{E r}}^{3+}$ strongly depend on their size [31]. Owing to both the confinement effect on the spatial distribution of phonon modes and a reduced spectral density of phonon states in $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanocrystals, an increase of the ratio between the intensities of green and red emission bands was observed with a decrease of their size. In our previous work [20],we observed novel UC luminescent switching between 450, 480.and800nmfrom $\mathrm{Y b^{3+}\mathrm{-}T m^{3+}}$ co-doped $\beta–NaYF_4$ nanorods by adjusting the pump-power densities of 980 nm LD. Unfortunately.little is known about the infl uence of the pump-power densities on the white color output in the triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanostructures 

 * Corresponding author at: Laboratory for Quantum Engineering and Micro-Nano Energy Technology and Faculty of Materials and Optoelectronic Physics,Xiangtan University,Hunan 411105,China.Tel.:+86 731 58292 113;fax:+86 731 58292 468.E-mail address:ylwxtu@xtu.edu.cn (L.W.Yang).


 [18].In this investigation,we fabricate $\mathrm{Y b^{3+}\mathrm{-H o^{3+}\mathrm{-T m^{3+}}}}$ (YHT) and $\mathrm{\dot{Y}\dot{b}^{3+}\mathrm{-}E r^{3+}\mathrm{-}T m^{3+}}$ (YET) triply-doped hexagonal $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods with bright white UC emission via a hydrothermal method. The dependences of the white color output on the pump-power densities in the two systems under the excitation of a 980 nm LD are investigated in detail.

Fig. 1a shows the typical TEM image of the $\mathrm{{L n}^{3+}\mathrm{{-}\mathrm{{d o p e d}}}}$ NaYF4nanorods, revealing a morphology with a diameter of about 55 nm and length of about 300 nm. Fig, 1b shows the electron diffraction pattern (SAeD) of single nanorod demonstrating unambiguously the single-crystalline nature. Fig. 1c is the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of a nanorod showing lattice fringes with the d-spacing of 3.05 and 2.82 Å. The SAED pattern and HRTEM image can be readily indexed to the hexagonal phase of $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}.$ . The results are well consistent with the XRD results shown in Fig. 1d,in which the diffraction peaks are well indexed to $\beta–NaYF_4$ :(JCPDS 16-0334). Further energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDS) confirms that the main elemental components are Na, Y, F and Yb (not shownhere),being consistent withtheir real elemental components.


 Our investigation chasing RGB emission and white light generation commences with the study of UC emission in binary dopant systems. Fig. 2a depicts the typical UC spectra acquired from the $\mathrm{\boldmath~\overset{\circ}{Y b}}^{3+}/\mathrm{\boldmath{E r}}^{3+}$ (25:0.2 mol%) co-doped sample under excitation by a 980 nm LD. The spectra show strong green emission centered at 540 nm and 525 nm and red UC one at 650 nm as well as weak violet and infrared emissions at 407 nm and 800 nm. They can be attributed tothe $^2\mathrm{H}_{11/2}/^{4}\mathrm{S}_{3/2}\rightarrow{}^{4}\mathrm{I}_{15/2},\;^{4}\mathrm{F}_{9/2}\rightarrow{}^{4}\mathrm{I}_{15/2},\;^{2}\mathrm{H}_{9/2}\rightarrow{}^{4}\mathrm{I}_{15/2}$ and $^4\mathrm{I}_{9/2}\rightarrow{}^{4}\mathrm{I}_{15/2}$ transitions of $\mathbb{E}\mathrm{r}^{3\overset{\cdot}{+}}.$ ,respectively. Fig. 2b shows the typical UC spectra from the $\mathrm{Y b^{3+}/H o^{3+}}(25:0.2\mathrm{m o l}\%)$ )co-doped sample under excitation by a 980 nm LD. Strong green and red UC 

 emission bands centered at 540 nm and 650 nm are shown, together with weak blue and near-infrared emission around 485 nm and 750 nm. These bands can be respectively assigned to the ${}^{5}\mathrm{F}_{5}\rightarrow{}^{5}\mathrm{I}_{8},~{}^{4}\mathrm{S}_{2}/{}^{5}\mathrm{F}_{4}\rightarrow{}^{5}\mathrm{I}_{8},~{}^{5}\mathrm{F}_{3}\rightarrow{}^{5}\mathrm{I}_{8}$ and $^5\mathrm{I}_{4}^{^*}\rightarrow{}^{5}\mathrm{I}_{8}$ transitions of $\mathrm{H o}^{3+}$ ions. The populations of the $\operatorname{\mathsf{E r}}^{3+}\operatorname{\mathsf{o r}}\operatorname{\mathsf{H o}}^{3+}$ excited levels to emit the aforementioned UC bands can be readily achieved through successive ET processes from $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}$ to $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ ions or $\mathsf{H o}^{3+}\left[1\right]$ ,as demonstrated in Fig. 3. Fig. 2c shows the typical UC spectra of the $\mathsf{Y}\mathsf{b}^{3+}/$ $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ (25:0.25 mol%) co-doped sample. The intense blue emission bands at 451 and 478 nm correspond to the ${}^{1}D_{2}\rightarrow{}^{3}F_{4}$ and ${}^{1}G_{4}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{6}$ transitions of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ions, respectively. The populations of the $^1D_{2}$ and $^1G_{4}$ levels are accomplished respectively by threestep sequential ETs from the excited $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}\mathsf{t o}\mathrm{T}\dot{\mathrm{m}}^{3+}$ and cross relaxation(CR) of $^3F_{2}+{}^{3}H_{4}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{6}+{}^{1}D_{2}$ between the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ions (CR-I),which is a four-photon UC process [8]. Besides blue emissions,red emissions at 650 and 696 nm assigned to ${}^{3}F_{2}/{}^{3}F_{3}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{6}$ or ${}^{1}G_{4}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{4}/{}^{3}H_{5}$ ;transitions of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ions which intensely depend on pump-power densities in the following, and intense near-infrared emission at 800 nm corresponding to ${}^{3}H_{4}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{6}$ transitions of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ions are also recorded in this sample. It is worth mentioning that the RGB light color is readily visible to the naked eyes, even for pump-power as low as 10 mW. This is indicative of the high UC effi ciency of the co-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods.


 Based on the generation of RGB light color from the different codoped samples, it is possible to produce white emission by appropriate triple doping of $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+},\mathsf{T}\bar{\mathsf{m}}^{3+}$ ,and $\mathrm{\tt   Er}^{3+}\mathrm{\tt   or}~\mathrm{\tt   Ho}^{3+}$ of the NaYF4nanorods. Fig. 2d displays the UC spectra of YHT $(\mathsf{Y}\mathsf{b}^{3+}\mathrm{{:}\mathrm{{-}}}$ $\mathrm{Ho^{3+}:Tm^{3+}=25:0.75:1}$ and YET $\mathrm{(Yb^{3+}:Er^{3+}:Tm^{3+}=25:0.25:1)}$ triply-doped NaYF4 nanorods showing bright white emission (see the inset photographs) under excitation by a 980 nm LD. Compared to the spectra of the binary dopant systems, it is easy to assign the origin of all the emission bands in the triple-doped $\mathtt{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods. The blue emission around 475 and 450 nm and the red emission at 696 nm can be assigned to the intra-4f electronic transitions of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ions, and the green emission around 540 nm to the $\mathrm{H o}^{3+}$ ions or $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ ions, For the red emission at 650 nm, it should include the contributions from both $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{H o}^{3+}$ ions or $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ according to the change of fine spectral shape in the red band.

 Fig. 4a shows the UC spectra and corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram of 1931 together with the calculated color coordinates of the YET-triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods under excitation by a 980 nm LD with various outputs. The calculated color coordinates range from (0.396, 0.395)to (0.264, 0.281), which correspond to the laser pump-power densities from 1.75$\mathsf{W}/\mathsf{c m}^{2}$ to 16.13 W/cm2. The arrow shown in the inset indicates the changing trends of the color coordinates with increasing pump-power densities.These color coordinates fall exactly within the white region of 1931 CIE diagram. In particular, the color coordinates at a pumppower density of 5.34$\mathsf{W}/\mathsf{c m}^{2}$ is very close to the standard color coordinates (0.33, 0.33), suggesting their potential use in standard white light sources. At high pump-power densities, the color coordinates shift towards the blue region due to the fact that the intensity of the blue emission increases faster than the green and red UC ones. These results indicate that the simulated white color output can be modulated by adjusting the pump-power densities of the LD. Fig. 4b shows the UC spectra and corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram of 1931 together with the calculated color coordinates of the YHT-triply-doped NaYF4 nanorods under excitation by a 980 nm LD with various outputs ranging from 1.78 W/cm2 to 16.54W/cm2. Similar dependence of the color coordinates on pump-power densities can be observed. However, compared to the results obtained from the YET-triply-doped system, there are two obvious differences. The first is that the range of the calculated color coordinates is much narrower than that in the former under the same pump-power densities. The second one is that the pumppower density at which the calculated color coordinates are very 

 close to the standard color ones is 11.05 W/cm is higher than that in the YET-triply-doped system. The results imply that the response scope to the pump-power densities for white emission from the YHT-triply-doped system is much broader than that from 

 the YET-triply-doped system and the simulated white color output can be tuned even precisely. Therefore, promising applications would be expected for the white emission of the YHT-triply-doped NaYF4 nanostructures. Furthermore, our results suggest a new 

 strategy to fine-tune the UC emission, different from that by precisely controlling the combinations of $\mathtt{L n}^{3+}$ doping kinds and concentration [14] or by changing the size of $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanocrystals [31].

 To further clarify the UC mechanism and dependence of the calculated color coordinates on the pump-power densities, the power dependent UC behaviors of the fi ve visible emission bands are systematically investigated. Fig. 5a shows the log-log plot of the Uc emission intensity versus pump-power densities for the YHT-triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F_{4}}$ nanorods. The slopes of the linear fits are 3.11,$2.14,$ ,1.53,1.48, and 1.34 for the five observed UC bands under low pump-power densities. The results indicate that two pump photons are necessary to produce the UC emission bands centered at 650,540 and 696 nm, while three and four photons are needed to produce the blue UC emission bands at 475 and 450 nm. They are consistent with previous reports concerning cubic $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4},\mathrm{Y}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ ！and $\mathtt{B a Y F}_{5}$ nanocrystals [10,14,32]. Furthermore, the slopes of the blue and green UC bands as well as red emission band at 650 nm tend to decrease. This saturation effect can be attributed to the two factors [33-35]. The first one is saturation of $\mathsf{^{\prime}Y b}^{3+}$ absorption at high pump-power and the second may be related to the saturation of the excited states of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}(^{3}\mathrm{F}_{4}),\mathrm{H o}^{3+}(^{5}\mathrm{I}_{6})$ ,and $\mathrm{H o}^{3+}(^{5}\mathrm{I}_{7})$ due to the efficient ET processes from $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}\mathsf{t o}\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ or $\mathrm{H o}^{3+}$ ions [33,34].However, the slope of the red emission band at 696 nm changes abnormally. For the abnormal change, we consider that it would originate from that at the low pump-power densities, it comes mainly from the ${}^{3}F_{3}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{6}$ transitions, which is a two-phonon process, but at high pump-power densities it does from the $^1G_{4}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{5}$ j transition of the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ,which is a three-phonon process. Fig. 5b shows the log-log plot of the UC emission intensity versus the pump-power obtained from the YET-triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods.Compared with the results in the YHT-triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods, saturation-effect-induced decrease of slope yaluesfor blue and green emissions, and high pump-power-induced transition from $\widetilde{^3F_{2}}\rightarrow{^3H_{6}}\mathrm{~\sf{t o}~}^{1}\widetilde{G_{4}}\rightarrow{^3H_{5}}$ to emit 696 nm photon also exist.Here, we specifically focus on the photon number for blue emission at low pump-power densities. The slopes of 1.89 and 2.66 indicate that the two and three pump-photon processes are necessary to produce the UC emissions at 475 and 450 nm, which is inconsistent with the results in the YHT-triply-doped systems. The results imply that the pump condition for the excited states of $^3H_{4}$ being saturated can be realized more readily in the YET-triply-doped systems. Considering that the ratio of $\mathsf{Y}\widetilde{\mathsf{b}}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ are same imply

 ing that the ET rate between $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ would be equal in both systems, we suggest that the CR between $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{T m}^{3\dot{+}}$ play a key role.


 There are many resonant CR processes for the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ and $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ ions. Previously, Chen et al. [9] found that by controlling the CR processes between the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ and the $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ ions, the red UC emission of $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ could be enhanced while blue UC emission of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ from transparent glass ceramics embedded with YET triply-doped $\mathtt{Y F}_{3}$ nanocrystals was reduced. It is believed that the nearly resonant CR process of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}{:}^{3}H_{6}+\mathrm{E r}^{3+}{:}^{2}I_{9/2}\rightarrow\mathrm{T m}^{3+}{:}^{3}H_{4}+\mathrm{E r}^{3+}{:}^{4}\bar{I}_{15/2}$ (CR-II in Fig. 3) is responsible for the saturation-effect inducing decrease in the required photon number to emit blue emissions at 450 nm and $475$ nm since this CR process will increase the population of the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}~^{3}H_{4}$ excited states to make it saturated more easily. As a result, the intensity of blue emissions relative to the green and red UC ones from the YET-triply-doped systems increases rather faster than that from the YHT-triply-doped systems. It should be pointed out that the CR-II process increases the lifetime of the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}{:}^{3}\mathrm{H}_{4}$ state in the YET-triply-doped system compared to the $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}-\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ co-doped system. It has been confirmed very recently in the YET-triply doped dielectric $\mathrm{{\tt{B a T i O}}}_{3}$ nanocrystals [15].

 Here, we also compare our results about UC white emission to the previously reported from other hosts such as glass ceramics containing $\mathsf{P b}_{1}$ ${}_{x}\mathsf{C d}_{x}\mathsf{F}_{2}$ or YF3nanocrystals,$\Upsilon_{2}\ensuremath{\boldsymbol{\mathrm{O}}}_{3}$ nanocrystals, Lu3$\mathsf{G a}_{5}\mathsf{O}_{12}$ :nanocrystals,$\mathrm{L u}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanocrystals,cubic $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanocrystals,and dielectric $\mathrm{{\tt{B a T i O}}}_{3}$ nanocrystals. In our experiments, we observe strong UC emission at 450 nm of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ions and this has seldom been observed from these aforementioned hosts. Generally, the UC process to populate the upper excited $^1D_{2}$ .level of $\mathrm{T m}^{3\overset{\circ}{+}}$ ions for the 450 nm emission occurs with a small probability because it involves a four-photon process, Therefore, it can be inferred that the UC effi ciency in hexagonal $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ should be higher than that reported for these various hosts. Moreover, the range of the white color output should be broader due to the contribution of the blue emission at 450 nm from hexagonal $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods.

question: What does Figure 2(a) depict?
answer: Figure 2(a) depicts the upconversion spectra from a \(\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}/\mathrm{Er}^{3+}\) co-doped \(\mathrm{NaYF}_{4}\) sample, showing strong green emissions at 540 nm and 525 nm, red emission at 650 nm, and weaker emissions at 407 nm and 800 nm under excitation by a 980 nm laser diode.

The first is that the range of the calculated color coordinates is much narrower than that in the former under the same pump-power densities. The second one is that the pumppower density at which the calculated color coordinates are very 

 close to the standard color ones is 11.05 W/cm is higher than that in the YET-triply-doped system. The results imply that the response scope to the pump-power densities for white emission from the YHT-triply-doped system is much broader than that from 

 the YET-triply-doped system and the simulated white color output can be tuned even precisely. Therefore, promising applications would be expected for the white emission of the YHT-triply-doped NaYF4 nanostructures. Furthermore, our results suggest a new 

 strategy to fine-tune the UC emission, different from that by precisely controlling the combinations of $\mathtt{L n}^{3+}$ doping kinds and concentration [14] or by changing the size of $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanocrystals [31].

 To further clarify the UC mechanism and dependence of the calculated color coordinates on the pump-power densities, the power dependent UC behaviors of the fi ve visible emission bands are systematically investigated. Fig. 5a shows the log-log plot of the Uc emission intensity versus pump-power densities for the YHT-triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F_{4}}$ nanorods. The slopes of the linear fits are 3.11,$2.14,$ ,1.53,1.48, and 1.34 for the five observed UC bands under low pump-power densities. The results indicate that two pump photons are necessary to produce the UC emission bands centered at 650,540 and 696 nm, while three and four photons are needed to produce the blue UC emission bands at 475 and 450 nm. They are consistent with previous reports concerning cubic $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4},\mathrm{Y}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ ！and $\mathtt{B a Y F}_{5}$ nanocrystals [10,14,32]. Furthermore, the slopes of the blue and green UC bands as well as red emission band at 650 nm tend to decrease. This saturation effect can be attributed to the two factors [33-35]. The first one is saturation of $\mathsf{^{\prime}Y b}^{3+}$ absorption at high pump-power and the second may be related to the saturation of the excited states of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}(^{3}\mathrm{F}_{4}),\mathrm{H o}^{3+}(^{5}\mathrm{I}_{6})$ ,and $\mathrm{H o}^{3+}(^{5}\mathrm{I}_{7})$ due to the efficient ET processes from $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}\mathsf{t o}\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ or $\mathrm{H o}^{3+}$ ions [33,34].However, the slope of the red emission band at 696 nm changes abnormally. For the abnormal change, we consider that it would originate from that at the low pump-power densities, it comes mainly from the ${}^{3}F_{3}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{6}$ transitions, which is a two-phonon process, but at high pump-power densities it does from the $^1G_{4}\rightarrow{}^{3}H_{5}$ j transition of the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ,which is a three-phonon process. Fig. 5b shows the log-log plot of the UC emission intensity versus the pump-power obtained from the YET-triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods.Compared with the results in the YHT-triply-doped $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods, saturation-effect-induced decrease of slope yaluesfor blue and green emissions, and high pump-power-induced transition from $\widetilde{^3F_{2}}\rightarrow{^3H_{6}}\mathrm{~\sf{t o}~}^{1}\widetilde{G_{4}}\rightarrow{^3H_{5}}$ to emit 696 nm photon also exist.Here, we specifically focus on the photon number for blue emission at low pump-power densities. The slopes of 1.89 and 2.66 indicate that the two and three pump-photon processes are necessary to produce the UC emissions at 475 and 450 nm, which is inconsistent with the results in the YHT-triply-doped systems. The results imply that the pump condition for the excited states of $^3H_{4}$ being saturated can be realized more readily in the YET-triply-doped systems. Considering that the ratio of $\mathsf{Y}\widetilde{\mathsf{b}}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ are same imply

 ing that the ET rate between $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ would be equal in both systems, we suggest that the CR between $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{T m}^{3\dot{+}}$ play a key role.


 There are many resonant CR processes for the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ and $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ ions. Previously, Chen et al. [9] found that by controlling the CR processes between the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ and the $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ ions, the red UC emission of $\mathtt{E r}^{3+}$ could be enhanced while blue UC emission of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ from transparent glass ceramics embedded with YET triply-doped $\mathtt{Y F}_{3}$ nanocrystals was reduced. It is believed that the nearly resonant CR process of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}{:}^{3}H_{6}+\mathrm{E r}^{3+}{:}^{2}I_{9/2}\rightarrow\mathrm{T m}^{3+}{:}^{3}H_{4}+\mathrm{E r}^{3+}{:}^{4}\bar{I}_{15/2}$ (CR-II in Fig. 3) is responsible for the saturation-effect inducing decrease in the required photon number to emit blue emissions at 450 nm and $475$ nm since this CR process will increase the population of the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}~^{3}H_{4}$ excited states to make it saturated more easily. As a result, the intensity of blue emissions relative to the green and red UC ones from the YET-triply-doped systems increases rather faster than that from the YHT-triply-doped systems. It should be pointed out that the CR-II process increases the lifetime of the $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}{:}^{3}\mathrm{H}_{4}$ state in the YET-triply-doped system compared to the $\mathsf{Y b}^{3+}-\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ co-doped system. It has been confirmed very recently in the YET-triply doped dielectric $\mathrm{{\tt{B a T i O}}}_{3}$ nanocrystals [15].

 Here, we also compare our results about UC white emission to the previously reported from other hosts such as glass ceramics containing $\mathsf{P b}_{1}$ ${}_{x}\mathsf{C d}_{x}\mathsf{F}_{2}$ or YF3nanocrystals,$\Upsilon_{2}\ensuremath{\boldsymbol{\mathrm{O}}}_{3}$ nanocrystals, Lu3$\mathsf{G a}_{5}\mathsf{O}_{12}$ :nanocrystals,$\mathrm{L u}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanocrystals,cubic $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanocrystals,and dielectric $\mathrm{{\tt{B a T i O}}}_{3}$ nanocrystals. In our experiments, we observe strong UC emission at 450 nm of $\mathrm{T m}^{3+}$ ions and this has seldom been observed from these aforementioned hosts. Generally, the UC process to populate the upper excited $^1D_{2}$ .level of $\mathrm{T m}^{3\overset{\circ}{+}}$ ions for the 450 nm emission occurs with a small probability because it involves a four-photon process, Therefore, it can be inferred that the UC effi ciency in hexagonal $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ should be higher than that reported for these various hosts. Moreover, the range of the white color output should be broader due to the contribution of the blue emission at 450 nm from hexagonal $\mathrm{N a Y F}_{4}$ nanorods.

The chart displays four subplots (a, b, c, d) showing room temperature upconversion (UC) spectra under excitation at 580 nm for different NaYF₄-based materials. Subplot (a) shows the UC spectra for Yb³⁺/Er³⁺ co-doped NaYF₄, with emission peaks at 520 nm (²H₁₁/₂ → ⁴I₁₅/₂) and 650 nm (⁴S₃/₂ → ⁴I₁₅/₂). Subplot (b) presents spectra for Yb³⁺/Ho³⁺ co-doped NaYF₄, showing emission peaks at 450 nm (⁵F₄ → ⁵I₈) and 650 nm (⁵F₅ → ⁵I₈). Subplot (c) illustrates spectra for Yb³⁺/Tm³⁺ co-doped NaYF₄, with emission peaks at 450 nm (³H₄ → ³F₄) and 650 nm (³H₄ → ³F₄). Subplot (d) includes the UC spectra for Yb³⁺/Tm³⁺ co-doped NaYF₄ along with corresponding digital photographs of the emission under excitation, showing blue and green emission.  Size 10 crochet thread Size 7 steel hook

R5: ch1, turn, sl st in first 3 stitches, ch1, sc in same st as last sl st. sc in next 4 st. do not 
work in last 2 stitches(4 sc)

R6: repeat row 3 (6 sc)

R7: repeat row 4 (8 sc)

R9: ch1, turn,sl in first 3 stitches, ch1,sc in same st aas last sl st.,sc in next 6 stitches.

R11: repeat row 5 (10 sc)

R12: repeat row 8 (12 sc)

R14: ch 1, turn. Sl in first 7 sc. Ch 1, sc in same st as last sl. Sc in next sc. Leave last 6
unworked(2 sc)

事件：2023年10月30日，福莱特发布2023年三季报，2023年前三季度公司实现营业收入158.85亿元，同比+41.64%；归母净利润19.69亿元，同比+30.86%；扣非归母净利润19.46亿元，同比+32.44%。其中Q3营业收入62.06亿元，同比+58.72%，环比+43.9%；归母净利润8.84亿元，同比+76.19%，环比+54.3%；扣非后净利润8.76亿元，同比+77.25%，环比+53.4%。

 光伏需求超预期，带动公司出货高增：2023年前三季度，受益于光伏组件价格进一步下行，装机需求超预期增长，据国家能源局，前三季度我国新增光伏装机达128.9GW，同比增145%，同时公司新增产能点火，带动公司光伏玻璃销量高增，驱动营收超预期增长。


 供需结构优化叠加原材料成本下行，玻璃盈利进一步回升：据我们测算，三季度光伏玻璃行业过剩产能比例进一步缩小至3%，供需逐步归于平衡的支撑下，玻璃价格坚挺；成本端来看，三季度主要原材料纯碱价格高位回落，以及天然气价格回归相对低位。供需结构优化叠加成本下行，带动公司盈利进一步改善。三季度公司毛利率回升至24.5%，环比提升4.6pct，回升至22年以来最高水平。

 ➢盈利预测及估值：我们预计23-25年光伏玻璃出货8.41/10.10/12.12亿平，营收分别为213.69/252.61/298.96亿元、归母净利分别为29.4/39.4/47.6亿元，当前股价对应23-25年PE20.7/15.4/12.8倍，公司作为光伏玻璃龙头企业，将继续凭借成本优势、管控能力穿越周期，拉开与同业盈利差距，继续巩固龙头地位，给予2024年20倍PE，对应目标价33.6元/股，维持“买入”评级。

A classic Paul Simon tune that ll of us who are in our 50s probably remember well the has lyrics that go something like this: "We work our jobs, collect our pay, believe we're gliding down the highway, when in fact we're slip sliding away. Slip sliding away...slip sliding away . . . you know, the nearer your destination, the more you're slip sliding away."

 I wonder if Jesus ever feels that way about you. I'm sure He probably feels that way about me at times, because there is a very subtle thing that happens in this pilgrimage.


 It is so subtle, you might not even notice it. We start out at the very beginning of our Christian lives when we go to the Cross, embrace those old rugged timbers, and feel the sin and weight of our guilt eternally removed. It starts out as a loving relationship with a Person,and it is driven by relationship.

 And yet slowly but surely it ends up being more like a ritual than a relationship. What starts out as a matter of the heart slowly but surely becomes a matter of habit.What was once a devotion becomes a duty.

L his issue of Veritas off ers you one of the "best of the best" messages delivered here on campus in our own historic Chafer Chapel. Dr. Joseph M. Stowell, president of Moody Bible Institute, delivered this edition's message.

 Joe encouraged our students and faculty at the 2000 Nathan D. Maier Memorial Series in 

 Bible Exposition last fall as he preached an impassioned message on something we all need to hear about: falling in love with Jesus over and over again, every day.


 I was so challenged by this message personally.

 You see, in the world of top-flight biblical scholarship, there may be the temptation to forget the point of that knowledge,which is to change hearts.


 Our desire at Dallas Seminary is for God to protect us—to ensure that intellectual prowess doesn't turn into intellectual pride. We don't want to produce mere thinkers, but, rather, leaders who know how to apply what they know in a way that changes their lives and the lives of others.


 As always, I urge you to pray for DTS, and my specific request in light of my good friend Joe Stowell's riveting message is for you to pray that we all—faculty, staff, and students—continue to have our hearts filled with Christ as well as our minds filled with Scripture.

 Your prayers are so crucial. God works through the prayers and financial support of committed Christians like you as He uses DTS to provide pastors, ministers, missionaries, and other leaders for His Church.


 God bless you as you read Joe Stowell's message about the heart, and apply it to yours.

question: What is the title of this article?
answer: Self-Coherent Multisymbol Detection of Optical Differential Phase-Shift Keying.

prediction.answer: Self-Coherent Multisymbol Detection of Optical Differential Phase-Shift Keying

retrieved_documents.content:
Moshe Nazarathy, Senior Member, IEEE, Xiang Liu, Senior Member, IEEE, Louis Christen, Member, IEEE,Yannick K. Lize, Student Member, IEEE, Student Member, OSA, and Alan E. Willner, Fellow, IEEE, Fellow, OSA 

 Abstract—This paper treats the recently emerging self-coherent multisymbol decision-feedback-aided detection techniques for optical differential phase-shift keyed (DPSK) transmission based on both an optical preprocessing approach and an electronic postprocessing approach. We present in detail i) the generation of a synthetic low phase-noise effective local oscillator out of the modulated optical data itself, and its estimation theory interpretation;ii) the role of polyphase (interleaved) DPSK in the optical preprocessing approach in easing the decision feedback realization requirements; ii) detection of M-ary DPSK modulation formats for general M, and differential quadrature phase-shift keving in particular; iv) three alternative integrated-optoelectronic receiver implementations for the optical preprocessing approach detailing the structures of the interferometric front-end and the high-speed electronic analog and digital building blocks; and v) the principle,and alternative implementations, of the digital postprocessing approach to self-coherent detection. We finally compare the capabilities of the emerging self-coherent detection and digital coherent detection techniques.
Index Terms—Coherent communication, decision feedback,differential phase-shift keying (DPSK), integrated optics, multisymbol DPSK, optical communication, phase modulation.

The principle of operation, briefly introduced in [2]for DF-aided MS-BDPSK, is now reviewed and extended to M-ary DPSK. Synthetic LO generation is interpreted in terms of estimation theory as well as intuitively accounting for it in terms of a phase-noise reduction effect due to averaging independent noisy samples over multiple symbols. The relevant parameters are the phase constellation size M (in particular,$M=2$ for DBPSK and $M\;=\;4$ forDQPSK)and D,themultisymbol sliding window size in chips, with the kth rightmost chip representing the $\mathrm{{}^{\mathrm{{}^{\mathrm{{}^{\mathrm{{}^{\mathrm{{s i g n a l}}}}}}}}}}$ while the preceding $D{-}1$ chips, ranging from $k-D+1\quad\mathrm{t o}\quad k{-}1$ , are used to synthesize the improved reference (ref). As receiver complexity rises upon increasing these parameters, we typically consider $M=2,4,8,\mathrm{\mathrm{\mathrm{\text--}i.e.}}$ ,DBPSK, DQPSK, and $8-\mathrm{P S K}\mathrm{\text--o v e r}$ a window of $D=3,4,5$ chips. Notice that $D=2$ : designates conventional DPSK to be henceforth referred to as two-symbol DPSK (2S-DPSK).

 As discussed in [2] and [3], the noise variance of the DPSK phase difference decision statistic is the sum of PN variances of the two adjacent symbols—the information and reference have the same signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), degrading self-homodyne by about 3 dB relative to homodyne PSK (the gap is reduced to 2.4 dB at bit error rate (BER) of $10^{-3}$ due to the degrading effect of amplitude noise on PSK). In principle, if a precise estimate of the phase of the last symbol reference were available.the DPSK performance would coincide with that of coherent homodyne PSK since the variance of the PN difference would be cut in half, once one of the two terms in this difference is rendered perfect. By feeding back the previous, mostly correctly decoded, signals, a better phase reference is extracted and effectively used to reduce the PN of the previous (reference) symbol.In essence, the estimate of the phase reference symbol, generated out of previous observations and decision feedback, better approximates the perfect LO available in actual coherent detection. These intuitive statements may be rigorously formalized by applying estimation theory to derive an ML estimate $\hat{\tilde{A}}_{k-1}$ of the ideal noiseless reference $\tilde{A}_{k-1}$ as a function of the vector of past observations $[\tilde{r}_{k-D+1},\ldots,\tilde{r}_{k-2},\tilde{r}_{k-1}]$ , assuming the linear channel model $\tilde{r}_{k}=\tilde{A}_{k}e^{j\theta}+\tilde{n}_{k}$ ,. Using the recursive rotation relation (6) and the fact that the noise is additive white Gaussian, the ML estimate comes out as a linear combination of past observations, with coefficients given by the cumulative rotations 


 \begin{array}{r l}{\hat{A}_{k-1}\equiv\tilde{R}_{k-1}=\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{D-1}\hat{s}_{[k-m,k-1]}\tilde{r}_{k-m}}\\ &{=\tilde{r}_{k-1}+\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-1}\tilde{r}_{k-2}+\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-1}\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-2}\tilde{r}_{k-3}}\\ &{\quad+\dots+\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-1}\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-2}\dots\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-D+2}\tilde{r}_{k-D+1}.}\end{array}

 Actually this estimate should have been expressed in terms of the past transmitted rotation symbols $\{\tilde{s}_{k-m}\}_{m=1}^{D-1}$ (without hat)to which the RX does not have access: however, the RX resorts instead to the slicer estimates $\left\{\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k\_m}\right\}_{m=1}^{D\_1}$ representing 

 the past decisions, which coincide with the transmitted rotation symbols $\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k\beth m}=\tilde{s}_{k\beth m}$ as long as the decisions are correct. The error propagation impact of incorrect decisions was shown both theoretically [2] and in simulations [2], [19] to be small.

 The estimate $\tilde{R}_{k-1}$ may be viewed as an improved reference or a synthetic self-coherent LO, generated in terms of prior observations and prior decisions and fed back to generate the improved reference. The angle $\angle\tilde{R}_{k-1}$ provides a better phase referencethan $\angle\tilde{r}_{k-1}$ (the noisy phase of the previous chip,used as self-homodyne reference in standard DPSK). For intuitive insight into the reference improvement, first specialize to $2\mathsf{S}\mathrm{-D P S K}\;(D=\mathrm{2})$ ,in which case $\tilde{R}_{k\perp1}=\tilde{r}_{k\perp1}$ ,i.e., the estimate simply reduces to the last reference symbol.

 Next consider $D~=~3$ ,in which case $\tilde{R}_{k-1}=\tilde{r}_{k-1}+$ $\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-1}\tilde{r}_{k-2}$ :

 In the noiseless case, we set $\tilde{n}_{k}=0,\mathrm{i.e.,}\tilde{r}_{k}=\tilde{A}_{k}=\langle\tilde{r}_{k}\rangle$ yielding for the noiseless estimate 

 \left\langle\tilde{R}_{k-1}\right\rangle=\tilde{A}_{k-1}+\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-1}\tilde{A}_{k-2}=\tilde{A}_{k-1}+\tilde{A}_{k-1}=2\tilde{A}_{k-1}.

 The mean of the improved reference has doubled in length, i.e.,quadrupled in power. Its noise component is $\tilde{n}_{k-1}+\hat{\tilde{s}}_{k-1}\tilde{n}_{k-2}$ with double the variance relative to $\tilde{r}_{k-1}$ ,resulting in doubled SNR. For general D, the noiseless or mean value of the estimate is 


 \begin{array}{r}{\left\langle\tilde{R}_{k-1}\right\rangle=\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{D-1}\prod_{l=1}^{m-1}\tilde{s}_{k-m+l}\tilde{A}_{k-m}=\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{D-1}\hat{\tilde{s}}_{[k-m,k=1]}\left\langle\tilde{r}_{k-m}\right\rangle}\\ {=\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{D-1}\hat{\tilde{s}}_{[k-m,k-1]}\tilde{A}_{k=m}=\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{D=1}\tilde{A}_{k=1}=(D-1)\tilde{A}_{k=1}.}\end{array}

 The $D-1$ identical mean-value phasors add up coherently,i.e., the signal amplitude component of the reference is coherently reinforced to $D-1$ times the noiseless phasor $\tilde{A}_{k-1}$ (the mean of the regular DPSK reference). In the noisy case, the received phasors $\tilde{r}_{k-D+1},\tilde{r}_{k-D+2},\dots,\tilde{r}_{k-2},\tilde{r}_{k-1}$ are all rotated in approximate alignment with $\tilde{r}_{k-1}$ (only perturbed by the PN);hence the signal amplitude is coherently reinforced to α$^{D-1}$ ,whereas the noise contributions add up incoherently on a root mean square basis $\propto\sqrt{D-1}$ , assuming uncorrelated successive phase-noise samples. The power SNR grows linearly in the number of chips $D–1$ ,which take part in forming the improved reference, with $A_{c}$ the magnitude of the transmitted symbols:

 \begin{aligned}\mathrm{SNR}\{\tilde{R}_{k-1}\}=\frac{\left|\left\langle\tilde{R}_{k-1}\right\rangle\right|^2}{\mathrm{Var}\tilde{R}_{k-1}}=\frac{[A_c(D-1)]^2}{(D-1)2\sigma^2}\\=(D-1)\frac{A_c^2}{2\sigma^2}=(D-1)\mathrm{SNR}\{\tilde{r}_{k-1}\}.\end{aligned} . (19)

 As the improved reference $\tilde{R}_{k-1}$ has the same mean angle as that of $\tilde{r}_{k-1}$ , except that it is quieter, it is advantageous to replace $\tilde{r}_{k-1}$ by $\tilde{R}_{k-1}$ in the RX processing. The improved receiver structure is designed to extract out of the current and $D–1$ past received symbols an improved sufficient statistic $\tilde{r}_{k}\tilde{R}_{k-1}^{*}$ instead of $\tilde{r}_{k}\tilde{r}_{k-1}^{*}$ . This amounts to mixing the current observation $\tilde{r}_{k}$ with the synthetic LO $\tilde{R}_{k-1}$ ._, synthesized out of the 

 (17)

 (18)

 (16)

 . (19)

 D-1 past observations, generating the phase difference estimates $\overline{{\angle\tilde{r}_{k}-\angle\tilde{R}_{k-1}}}$ . The ML receiver (1) over the extended multisymbol window uses a modified decision variable $w_{k}^{(\alpha)}$ 

 \begin{array}{r l}{w_{k}^{(\alpha)}\equiv\mathrm{R e}\left\{\tilde{r}_{k}\tilde{R}_{k-1}^{*}e^{-j\Delta\mu_{k}^{(\alpha)}}\right\}=\mathrm{R e}\left\{\tilde{q}_{k}\tilde{s}_{k}^{(\alpha)*}\right\}}\\ {=|\tilde{r}_{k}|\left|\tilde{R}_{k-1}\right|\cos\left(\angle\tilde{r}_{k}-\angle\tilde{R}_{k-1}-\Delta\mu_{k}^{(\alpha)}\right)}\end{array}

 where $\tilde{q}_{k}$ is now redefined as the mixing product of the observation $\tilde{r}_{k}$ with the improved reference $\tilde{R}_{k-1}$ rather than $\tilde{r}_{k-1}$ 

 \tilde{q}_{k}\equiv\tilde{r}_{k}\tilde{R}_{k-1}^{*}.

 These abstract formulas are embodied in the DF MC-DPSK optimal receiver block diagram realizations treated in this paper.For high SNR, the noisy phase of the ref is given by the quadrature noise over the length of the phasor (with the second formula for 2S-DPSK)


 \angle\tilde{R}_{k-1}\approx\mu_{k-1}+\frac{\sum\limits_{m=1}^{D-1}\tilde{n}_{k-m}^{i m}}{A_{c}(D-1)},\quad\angle\tilde{r}_{k-1}\approx\frac{\tilde{n}_{k-1}^{i m}}{A_{c}}.

 The PN variance equals the inverse of the SNR 

 \begin{aligned}\operatorname{Var}\{\angle\tilde{R}_{k-1}\}&\approx\frac{\sigma^{2}/A_{c}^{2}}{D-1}=\operatorname{SNR}^{-1}\left\{\tilde{R}_{k-1}\right\}\\&=\frac{\operatorname{SNR}^{-1}\left\{\tilde{r}_{k-1}\right\}}{D-1}.\end{aligned}

 The reduction in phase-noise variance relative to 2S-DPSK,called self-coherent gain, is seen to be equal to the number of chips $D{-}1$ in the reference window (for 2S-DPSK $\mathrm{G_{s c}=1)}$ )

 G_{\mathrm{S C}}\equiv\frac{\mathrm{V a r}\{\angle\tilde{r}_{k-1}\}}{\mathrm{V a r}\{\angle\tilde{R}_{k-1}\}}=D-1.

 This indicates that in the asymptotic limit $D\to\infty$ of a sufficiently large multisymbol window size, the DPSK phase reference tends to a perfectly clean effective optical LO, equivalet to an LO in coherent PSK homodyne, i.e. DF MC-DPSK performance tends to coherent homodyne.


 In summary, the DF MC-DPSK technique emulates coherent detection, attaining effective coherent homodyne behavior while avoiding the complexity of an actual LO laser and associated optical phase-locked loop. Such an SC phase detection scheme with improved reference (synthetic LO)(16)is in principle more immune against any PN source, be it the PN induced by the additive optical amplifier ASE, the nolinear PN induced in the fiber link by the Gordon–Mollenauer effect,or uncorrelated components in XPM-induced PN.

 The optoelectronic realizations treated next may always be interpreted as synthesizing a coherent LO out of the received signal, then mixing the received signal with the synthetic LO,though in certain cases the interpretation is implicit in equivalet mathematical transformations.

retrieved_documents.content:
L.E.C.A.P.-E.P.T./I.N.S.A.T.

 B.P. 676, 1080 Tunis Cedex, Tunisia 

 Slim.BenOthman@ept.rnu.tn,Meftah.Ghrissi@sotufem.com.tn,Ahmed.BenSalem@ept.rnu.tn,SlimBenSaoud@fulbrightweb.org 

 Abstract—Inrecent years, Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) have proven themselves capable of handling a wide variety of tasks, from relatively simple electrical system control functions to more complex, algorithmic operations. According to the controller complexity, the FPGA design can be achieved by mixed Software/Hardware solutions. However, in many cases, the FPGA-based controller architectures require extensive Real Time (RT) testing before applying in real conditions. Thus, this paper will propose new approach to test controller design by implementing real time motor emulator linked to its controller drive in the same System on Chip (SoC). The gain with using this new approach is the ability to push past the operational limits of a specific environment and to test fault conditions that would otherwise be damaging or dangerous for a real motor. The proposed work describes an FPGA HardCore single processing implementation of a RT motor control loop and then explores the viability and cost-performance of this FPGA design.

retrieved_documents.content: 4.Tension –is the force applied to string, rope, chain or cable. Its symbol is T.

ENHANCED SCIENCE 8

 EARL CRIS B.RIGGS TEACHER

There are two types of force, namely:

 Contact forces – forces where objects touch or contact with each other.

 Examples of contact forces:

 1.Applied  – a force given to a person or object by another person or object. Its symbol is F depending on who or what applies force to the object. If a boy applies a force to a wall, we denote it with FBoy. Refer to the figure below.

 ENHANCED SCIENCE Q1W1LESSON:FORCES 

 2.Friction – is the force acting against or opposite an object in contact with which makes the movement of the object t slow down.Friction always opposes the motion of an object.Its symbol is written as $\mathbf{F}_{f^{\bullet}}$ Air resistance denoted by $\mathbf{F}_{A I R}$ is an example of frictional force of the air against a flying kite,airplanes,parachutes or those in skydiving sports.For free-falling objects,this force is always considered negligible,meaning the magnitude is unnoticeable.


 3.Normal – is the force that acts perpendicular to the surface of the object in contact with. Its symbol is $\mathbf{F}_{\mathtt{N}}$

Investigate the relationship between the amount of force applied and the mass of the object to the amount of change in the object's motion.
(Week 1S8FE-Ia-15)


 ENHANCED SCIENCE Q1W1LESSON:FORCES 

 Figure 7 shows how force acts on a ball, but you need to be familiar with the following terms:

 magnitude - refers to the size or strength of the force. It is commonly expressed in Newton (N) for Meter-Kilogram-Second (MKS)system,Dyne (dyn) for Centimeter-Gram-Second (CGS) system and pounds (1bs) for FootPound-Second (FPS)system.In the International System of Units (SI),Newton is commonly used which is named after Sir Isaac Newton,an English  physicist and mathematician.


 direction 一points to where the object goes. The direction of the arrowhead indicates the direction of the force. The length of the arrow represents the amount of force (relative magnitude).


 point of application 一the location of where the force is applied.

 line of action – is the straight line passing through the point of application and is parallel to the direction of force.

Non-contact forces 一forces where objects do not touch or contact with each other.These forces act over a zone or area called field.


 Examples of non-contact forces:

 1.Gravitational (Fg) - is the force of attraction between two objects. In the case of the Earth, this gravitational force causes objects to fall down to the ground. It makes satellites and smaller objects stay in orbit near the more massive planets. Mass and distance of the two objects affect the gravitational force that holds them. The bigger the masses of the objects are, the bigger is the gravitational force between them. The closer the objects are, the greater is the gravitational force between them. The figure below illustrates gravitational force between the Earth and the Moon. Earth has bigger gravitational force over the Moon.

 华为mate50“向上捅破天”新技术发布，华为携手北斗三号系统搭载短报文功能，实现手机卫星通信技术的大众化应用。卫星通信是利用人造地球卫星作为中继站转发或反射无线电信号，在卫星通信地球站之间或地球站与航天器之间的通信。按照轨道高度，通信卫星主要包括LEO（低地球轨道）、MEO（中地球轨道）、GEO（地球静止轨道），北斗卫星是我国自研的地球同步轨道卫星。华为Mate50搭载卫星通信功能，将进一步带动北斗卫星相关产业链发展。建议关注相关标的。

question: What is the total number of figures and tables combined on pages 2 and 3?
What technique was used for analyzing receptor expression in the study?

retrieved_documents.content: Coxsackievirus and adenovirus receptor protein expression was detected by immunohistochemical staining of standard 5 m formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissue sections. The staining protocol has been described before8and uses a rabbit-polyclonal antibody (Ab72) directed against full-length CAR protein. During the course of the study, a new rabbit anti-CAR antibody (CAR H300,Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Santa Cruz, CA), became commercially available that showed significantly less unspecific staining than Ab72 in Western blot experiments (data not shown). The study was continued with this antibody at a dilution of 1:50. Semiquantitative assessment of CAR cell-surface expression was performed by two of the investigators (WMK and LF) by estimating the percentage of cells demonstrating CAR protein expression at the cell surface. In two cases, two differently differentiated tumor areas were found. In these cases,separate CAR expression scores were assigned for each tumor area.

Cast on 6x2=12 sts using Turkish cast on described above. Knit one round.

 Increase every second round like this: kl,ml, knit until I st before end of needle, ml, kl.Repeat on second needle.Increase until you have 
$12\times2=24\mathrm{s t}$ 


 Knit until you have knitted 21rounds all together.


 Prepare for the Elisabeth Zimmerman thumb, described on page 3,by knitting 6 sts.

 Using contrasting coloured yarn, knit 4 st. Slip those 4 sts back on the right needle,break contrasting yarn and continue knitting over the sts.(Second mitten; mirror thumb by knitting 2 sts, knit 4 in contrasting yarn, slip them back and continue knitting)Knit 7 more rounds.


 If preferred, pic up yarn in other colour and decrease for rib: k4, k2tog. Repeat until end of round.


 Knit a lxl rib until you find it long enough.


 Cast off using sewn bind off for Ixl rib,described on page 4.

question: What is the title on the second page?
answer: A Time to Say Thank You

prediction.answer: Pastor's Pondering: A Time to Say Thank You

retrieved_documents.content:
By Rev. Trey Little, Senior Pastor 

 "Time is significant because it is so rare. It is completely irretrievable. You can never repeat it or relive it. There is no such thing as a literal instant replay. That appears only on film.It travels alongside us every day, yet it has eternity wrapped up in it. Although this is true, time often seems relative, doesn't it? For example, two weeks on a vacation is not at all like two weeks on a diet. Also,some people can stay longer in an hour than others can in a week! Ben Franklin said of time,'.….that is the stuf life is made of.'Time forms life's building blocks. The philosopher William James once said,'The great use of life is to spend it for something that will outlast it." (Lloyd Cory, Quote Unquote).

 As I write this final newsletter article,I find myself very conscious of time. I realize the end of my time here at FPC is drawing near. I recognize that the time has come for me to say goodbye but I do so with the assurance that it is only for a time. It is also time for me to say "Thank You" to so many of the wonderful people of FPC.


 Thank you for the love and affirmation so many of you have shown my family and me during our time here.

 Thank you for the hospitality.

 Thank you for the generosity.

 Thank you for the prayers.

 Thank you to the many children who have drawn pictures of me and for me during worship—priceless treasures to say the least.


 Thank you to the wonderful staff of FPC for your incredible support and love and for making Jesus visible to me on a daily basis.


 Thank you for the various "fun gifts" that have made their way onto my book shelves.


 Thank you for the opportunities for growth.

 Thank you for the many laughs; and thank you for allowing me to share a few tears.


 Thank you for your patience.

 Thank you for reaching out to my kids and loving them and including them.


 Thank you for graciously encouraging my "sweet bride."

 Thank you for allowing me to be a part of your family;thank you for choosing to be a part of my family.

 Thank you for having us into your homes.

 Thank you for the many tangible gifts you have given us over the years but also thank you for the countless intangible gifts as well.


 Thank you for welcoming us here but also thank you for graciously releasing us to go where God is leading.

 The truth of the matter is,there is not enough TIME to adequately express our THANKS to the great people of First Presbyterian Church. Therefore, please know you all will remain in our hearts and prayers as we continue to lift up FPC to the Lord.We will pray for the Lord to continue to richly bless you as you continue living to make Jesus visible.

 Keep smilin' and keep lovin'!

 Peace,

 It's summer time! Are you planning a trip or two this year?Maybe a road trip along the interstate to a beach, a lake, or further afield?


 Wherever you go, you know you need directions. Whether it's a piece of paper or a GPS app on your smartphone,maps help orient us and guide us so we can arrive at our destination. And as we travel we look for road signs along the way so we can be sure we are headed in the right direction.


 God has given us "road signs" to steer us in the right direction through life—the 10 commandments. Sometimes called the "Decalogue" in Greek (for "10 words"), these commandments are incredibly relevant to us as followers of Jesus today! Living out the 10 commandments strengthens our faith from the inside out, so that we can live a fulfilled life of love, service, and discipleship as God has called us to do. In this way our "inner faith" is outwardly expressed.

 Beginning July 6,2014,join us on Innerfaith 10—a journey through the 10 Commandments. Together we will learn from the wisdom of our God as we continue to strive to 

 travel toward him and with him in our daily lives. Worship with us Sundays or, if you are traveling and can't make it to First Pres on Sundays, listen to the messages online at www.fpcsanantonio.org/media.


 July 6:God First (Exodus 20:1-6)

 July 13:God Bless (Exodus 20:7)

 July 20:God's Day (Exodus 20:8-11)

 July 27:God's Promise (Exodus 20:12)

 August 3:God's Life (Exodus 20:13)

 August 10:God's Love (Exodus 20:14)

 (Note:No contemporary worship service August 10 due to MoRanch church retreat weekend)


 August 17:God's Passion (Exodus 20:15)

 August 24:God's Truth (Exodus 20:16)

 August 31:God's Desire (Exodus 20:17)

retrieved_documents.content:
Masood Ghotbi and Majid Ebrahim-Zadeh 

 ICFO-Institut de Ciencies Fotoniques, Mediterranean Technology Park,08860 Castelldefels, Barcelona, Spain 

 Valentin Petrov, Pancho Tzankov and Frank Noack 

 Max-Born-Institute for Nonlinear Optics and Ultrafast Spectroscopy, 2A Max-Born-Str. D-12489 Berlin, Germany petrov@mbi-berlin.de 

 Abstract: We demonstrate efficient operation of a tunable femtosecond optical parametric amplifier based on $\mathrm{B i}\bar{\mathrm{B}}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{6}$ pumped at 800 nm by a 1 kHz Ti:sapphire regenerative amplifier. The idler wavelength coverage extends to beyond 3 m and the pulse duration at this wavelength is of the order of 110 fs. This new nonlinear borate crystal offers exceptionally high nonlinearity, making it a very promising candidate for power scaling of such frequency converters in the near-IR.
